## PAGE 00365: CSV Export Report Designer

- page_id: `209257223`
- space_key: `MD2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024x/pages/209257223/CSV+Export+Report+Designer
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2025-02-03T08:05:06.592+01:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > Report Wizard > Report Designer
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

In this section, you will learn how to export a Diagram Table in CSV format.

To export a Diagram Table in the CSV format using the Magic Report plugin

1. On the main menu, click Options>Environment . The Environment Options dialog opens.
2. In the Report Wizard option group, click Add to add a new row in the Configure template mappings table. [ATTACHMENT filename='Environment Options dialog.png']
3. In the Template mappings dialog, add . csv as the file extension and . txt as the Template type. [ATTACHMENT filename='Template mapping dialog.png']
4. Click OK .
5. Create a blank text file and copy the sample code below in the text file. = 0 || \$value.indexOf(',') >= 0)
#set($value = '"' + $s + '"')
#else
#foreach ($c in $value.toCharArray())
#if($c.getType($c)==15)
#set($value = '"' + $s + '"')
#break
#end
#end
#end
#end
#import("table", "com.nomagic.reportwizard.tools.DiagramTableTool")
#foreach($d in $table.filterDiagramTables($Diagram))
#set($colIds = $table.getColumnIds($d))$d.getName()
#foreach($te in $table.getColumnNames($d, $colIds))#formatValue($te)$value,#end
#foreach($re in $table.getRows($d))
#foreach($id in $colIds)#formatValue($table.getStringCellValue($d, $re, $id))$value,#end
#end
$table.clearTablesCache()
#end]]>
6. Save this text file with a CSV file extension.
7. Add the CSV template to the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Report Wizard dialog' space=''] and generate a CSV report.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>In this section, you will learn how to export a Diagram Table in CSV format.</p><p>To export a Diagram Table in the CSV format using the Magic Report plugin</p><hr /><ol><li>On the main menu, click <strong>Options&gt;Environment</strong>. The Environment Options dialog opens.</li><li>In the Report Wizard option group, click <strong>Add</strong> to add a new row in the Configure template mappings table.<br /><br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Environment Options dialog.png" /></ac:image><br /><br /></li><li>In the Template mappings dialog, add .<strong>csv </strong>as the file extension and .<strong>txt </strong>as the Template type.<br /><br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Template mapping dialog.png" /></ac:image><br /><br /></li><li>Click <strong>OK</strong>.</li><li>Create a blank text file and copy the sample code below in the text file.<br /><br /><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="b7c0570a-351b-45ba-b332-f2e8b9b49af3"><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[#macro(formatValue $s)
#set($value = $s)
#if($value.indexOf('"') >= 0 || \$value.indexOf(',') >= 0)
#set($value = '"' + $s + '"')
#else
#foreach ($c in $value.toCharArray())
#if($c.getType($c)==15)
#set($value = '"' + $s + '"')
#break
#end
#end
#end
#end
#import("table", "com.nomagic.reportwizard.tools.DiagramTableTool")
#foreach($d in $table.filterDiagramTables($Diagram))
#set($colIds = $table.getColumnIds($d))$d.getName()
#foreach($te in $table.getColumnNames($d, $colIds))#formatValue($te)$value,#end
#foreach($re in $table.getRows($d))
#foreach($id in $colIds)#formatValue($table.getStringCellValue($d, $re, $id))$value,#end
#end
$table.clearTablesCache()
#end]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro></li><li>Save this text file with a CSV file extension.</li><li>Add the CSV template to the <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Report Wizard dialog" /></ac:link> and generate a CSV report.</li></ol>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=136715240 space=MD2024x version=2 -->
## PAGE 00366: Custom diagram painters

- page_id: `136715240`
- space_key: `MD2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024x/pages/136715240/Custom+diagram+painters
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2024-02-13T08:22:01.922+01:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > Developer Guide > Working with diagrams
- labels: ['diagram-surface']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

The Open API provides a way to add your own custom diagram painters for painting some additional stuff on the diagram canvas. A good sample would be some highlighting in the diagram.

The painter can be added only into the opened diagram's *com.nomagic.magicdraw.uml.symbols.DiagramSurface*. Only the opened diagram has *DiagramSurface*. A closed diagram returns *null*.

###### Example of the code:

```java

```

```text

```

****You can find the code examples in *<installation_directory>\openapi\examples\customdiagrampainter*

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:layout><ac:layout-section ac:type="two_right_sidebar"><ac:layout-cell><div class="WordSection1"><p>The Open API provides a way to add your own custom diagram painters for painting some additional stuff on the diagram canvas. A good sample would be some highlighting in the diagram.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="cc78d5d3-78d1-479e-a45e-3b1a6bab7f5d"><ac:rich-text-body><p>The painter can be added only into the opened diagram's <em>com.nomagic.magicdraw.uml.symbols.DiagramSurface</em>. Only the opened diagram has <em>DiagramSurface</em>. A closed diagram returns <em>null</em>.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><h4>Example of the code:</h4><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="223a2e8d-b1dd-4546-80f1-c344a7640e85"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">java</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[Application.getInstance().addProjectEventListener(new ProjectEventListenerAdapter()
{
   public void projectOpened(Project project)
   {
      project.addPropertyChangeListener(new PropertyChangeListener()
      {
         public void propertyChange(PropertyChangeEvent evt)
         {
            if(evt.getPropertyName().equals(Project.DIAGRAM_OPENED))
            {
               DiagramPresentationElement diagram = Application.getInstance().getProject().getActiveDiagram();
               diagram.getDiagramSurface().addPainter(new DiagramSurfacePainter()
               {
                  public void paint(Graphics g, DiagramPresentationElement diagram)
                  {
                     g.setColor(Color.BLUE);
                     List symbols = diagram.getPresentationElements();
                     for (int i = 0; i < symbols.size(); i++)
                     {
                        PresentationElement o = (PresentationElement)symbols.get(i);
                        if( o instanceof ShapeElement)
                        {
                           Rectangle bounds = o.getBounds();
                           bounds.grow(5,5);
                           ((Graphics2D)g).draw(bounds);
                        }

                     }

                  };

               });

            }

         }

      });

   }

});]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><pre>    </pre></div><ac:structured-macro ac:name="info" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="149c6619-33cd-40ac-aa63-ffaeb054ed5d"><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong> </strong>You can find the code examples in <em>&lt;installation_directory&gt;\openapi\examples\customdiagrampainter</em></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></ac:layout-cell><ac:layout-cell><p><br /></p></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section></ac:layout>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=136715300 space=MD2024x version=1 -->
## PAGE 00367: Custom elements numbering

- page_id: `136715300`
- space_key: `MD2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024x/pages/136715300/Custom+elements+numbering
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-09-20T18:17:21.402+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > Developer Guide
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

The generic numbering open API provides two interfaces of different complexity. They allow a user to modify how the numbering process executes.

- The [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='IJavaNumberPart interface' space='MD2024x'] interface produces a single part of a number generated by the framework. It does not interfere with the execution of the framework, but simply provides a little piece of information included in the numbering.
- The [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='INumberingAction interface' space='MD2024x'] allows for generating complete numbers for sets of elements. All sorting and numbering is delegated to the implementing class. The framework will only register the generated numbers internally.

Classes that implement these interfaces can be set as the expression value of an Expression NumberPart. (see [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Numbering Scheme property values' space='MD2024x']).

These classes have to be specified with the complete package and class name, for example, *com.nomagic.magicdraw.autoid.sample.SquareNumberPart*.

#### PANEL: Related pages

Related pages

true

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:layout><ac:layout-section ac:type="two_right_sidebar"><ac:layout-cell><p>The generic numbering open API provides two interfaces of different complexity. They allow a user to modify how the numbering process executes.</p><ul><li>The <em><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="IJavaNumberPart interface" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[IJavaNumberPart]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link></em> interface produces a single part of a number generated by the framework. It does not interfere with the execution of the framework, but simply provides a little piece of information included in the numbering.</li><li>The <em><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="INumberingAction interface" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[INumberingAction]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link></em> allows for generating complete numbers for sets of elements. All sorting and numbering is delegated to the implementing class. The framework will only register the generated numbers internally.</li></ul><p>Classes that implement these interfaces can be set as the expression value of an Expression NumberPart. (see <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Numbering Scheme property values" /></ac:link>).<span style="color: rgb(255,0,0);"> </span></p><p>These classes have to be specified with the complete package and class name, for example, <em style="line-height: 1.4285715;">com.nomagic.magicdraw.autoid.sample.SquareNumberPart</em>.</p></ac:layout-cell><ac:layout-cell><ac:structured-macro ac:name="panel" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="918a5c6d-e796-417e-ae7a-a2c46c77d972"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Related pages</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="children" ac:schema-version="2" ac:macro-id="845f50c5-47fd-4d7e-b95e-17c259663b9e"><ac:parameter ac:name="all">true</ac:parameter></ac:structured-macro></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section></ac:layout>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=136715299 space=MD2024x version=2 -->
## PAGE 00368: Custom Legend Item Implementation

- page_id: `136715299`
- space_key: `MD2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024x/pages/136715299/Custom+Legend+Item+Implementation
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2024-02-13T08:39:02.512+01:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > Developer Guide
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

It is possible to create Java implementation describing what model element or presentation element should a Legend Item adorn in a diagram.

1. The Java class should be implemented following the very same rules as when [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Implementing a binary validation rule' space=''] . Each com.nomagic.magicdraw.annotation.Annotation object returned by the rule will cause the targeted model or presentation element to be adorned in the diagram (but only if the Legend is used in that diagram).
2. Specify the full class name as the value of "Implementation" property for the Legend Item in the modeling application.
3. Compile Java code, resulting in .class files possibly contained by a .jar file.
4. Ensure that your modeling tool can find and load these classes. You can place them in the program classpath or use the modeling tool plugins mechanism.

#### TIP: Example

Example

See *AdornComponentsOfInterestInDiagramValidator.java* example implementation in the *<program installation directory>\openapi\examples\legendItem*directory.

Also see: [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Legends' space='']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>It is possible to create Java implementation describing what model element or presentation element should a Legend Item adorn in a diagram.  </p><ol><li>The Java class should be implemented following the very same rules as when <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Implementing a binary validation rule" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[creating implementation for a validation rule]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>. Each <em>com.nomagic.magicdraw.annotation.Annotation</em> object returned by the rule will cause the targeted model or presentation element to be adorned in the diagram (but only if the Legend is used in that diagram).</li><li>Specify the full class name as the value of &quot;Implementation&quot; property for the Legend Item in the modeling application. </li><li>Compile Java code, resulting in .class files possibly contained by a .jar file.</li><li>Ensure that your modeling tool can find and load these classes. You can place them in the program classpath or use the modeling tool plugins mechanism.<br /><br /></li></ol><ac:structured-macro ac:name="tip" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="6964f9b3-6d3c-4d75-bec5-da99cd79962f"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Example</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>See <em>AdornComponentsOfInterestInDiagramValidator.java</em> example implementation in the <em>&lt;program installation directory&gt;\openapi\examples\legendItem </em>directory.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><br />Also see: <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Legends" /></ac:link></p>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=136715225 space=MD2024x version=1 -->
## PAGE 00369: Custom presentation elements rendering

- page_id: `136715225`
- space_key: `MD2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024x/pages/136715225/Custom+presentation+elements+rendering
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-09-20T18:17:13.702+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > Developer Guide > Working with diagrams > Presentation elements (symbols)
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

The custom symbol rendering API allows change a default default symbol view. This API includes:

1. Renderer provider API, which allows to provide a custom renderer for the specific presentation element.
2. Renderers, which can modify the default presentation element appearance.

In this section, we will review, how to register the specific presentation element views provider and give the example covering the custom renderers for the package, slots, and dependency link symbols.

****You can find the code examples in *<installation_directory>\openapi\examples\symbolrendering.*

#### PANEL: Related pages

Related pages

true

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

This API includes:</p><ol><li>Renderer provider API, which allows to provide a custom renderer for the specific presentation element.</li><li>Renderers, which can modify the default presentation element appearance.</li></ol><p>In this section, we will review, how to register the specific presentation element views provider and give the example covering the custom renderers for the package, slots, and dependency link symbols.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="info" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="149c6619-33cd-40ac-aa63-ffaeb054ed5d"><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong> </strong>You can find the code examples in <em>&lt;installation_directory&gt;\openapi\examples\symbolrendering.</em></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></ac:layout-cell><ac:layout-cell><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="panel" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="5400cec7-8f81-40d0-809c-38f6bac028a7"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Related pages</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="children" ac:schema-version="2" ac:macro-id="05088e21-c8f3-4865-8c3f-35850941b106"><ac:parameter ac:name="all">true</ac:parameter></ac:structured-macro></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section></ac:layout>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=136715183 space=MD2024x version=2 -->
## PAGE 00370: Custom profile implementation

- page_id: `136715183`
- space_key: `MD2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024x/pages/136715183/Custom+profile+implementation
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2024-02-13T08:41:34.713+01:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > Developer Guide > UML model > Working with stereotypes and tagged values
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

Instead of using low level *com.nomagic.uml2.ext.jmi.helpers.StereotypesHelper* API to access information about profile elements, it is possible to generate a profile implementation class out of the UML Profile model. This implementation class wraps all mapping, naming details with a nice Java API. MagicDraw comes with already generated profiles implementation for standard profiles. See [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Standard profiles implementation' space=''] page for details.

To create your own custom profile implementation you need to follow these steps

1. Install Development Tools plugin from Help->Resource Manager
2. Open UML Profile model with MagicDraw.
3. Generate profile implementation code using Tools->Development Tools->Generate Profile Class Implementation (wrappers) ...

Profile implementation class has these features

- There is a constant defined for every DataType, Enumeration name
- There is a getter for every DataType element defined in the profile
- Java enumeration is generated for every Enumeration element defined in the profile. This class has following features
  - There are methods to get enumeration literals by generated enumeration and vice versa
- Inner java class is generated for every Stereotype defined in the profile. This class has following features
  - There is a constant defined for Stereotype name
  - There are constants defined for all stereotype tags names
  - There is a setter/getter method available for every stereotype tag. Signature of these methods corresponds to tag multiplicity type and etc. No more need to work with strings and casting values to primitives as in StereotypesHelper case.
  - There is a check method to check if Element has specific stereotype applied
  - There is a getter for Stereotype element itself

It is highly recommended to assign URI to Profile element in the UML model before generation. URI is an unique Profile identifier and helps to avoid conflicts if model has several profiles with same name applied.

**Custom sample profile**

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

**Usages example**

```java

```

```text
Generated implementation class for this custom profile
```

```java
value)
      {
         Profiles.setValue(element, getStereotype(), getFriendsProperty(), value);
      }

      public void clearFriends(Element element)
      {
         Profiles.clearValue(element, getFriendsProperty());
      }

      public void addFriends(Element element, String value)
      {
         Profiles.addValue(element, getStereotype(), getFriendsProperty(), value);
      }

      public void removeFriends(Element element, String value)
      {
         java.util.List values = getFriends(element);
         values.remove(value);
         setFriends(element, values);
      }

      @SuppressWarnings("unchecked")
      public java.util.List getFriends(Element element)
      {
         return (java.util.List) Profiles.getValue(element, getFriendsProperty());
      }

      @Override
      protected void clear()
      {
         super.clear();
         country = null;
         friends = null;
      }

      @Override
      public boolean is(@CheckForNull Element element)
      {
         return element instanceof com.nomagic.uml2.ext.magicdraw.mdusecases.Actor &&
               _p.isTypeOf(element, getStereotype());
      }

      public static boolean isInstance(@CheckForNull Element element)
      {
         if (element instanceof com.nomagic.uml2.ext.magicdraw.mdusecases.Actor)
         {
            CustomProfile instance = getInstance(element);
            return instance.isTypeOf(element, instance.person().getStereotype());
         }
         return false;
      }
   }

   @Override
   protected Collection generatedGetAllElementWrappers()
   {
      Collection wrappers = new ArrayList<>();
      wrappers.add(personStereotype);
      return wrappers;
   }

   @Override
   protected Collection generatedGetAllStereotypes()
   {
      if (getProfile() != null)
      {
         final Collection stereotypes = new HashSet<>();
         stereotypes.add(personStereotype.getStereotype());
         return stereotypes;
      }
      return Collections.emptyList();
   }
}]]>
```

#### PANEL: Related pages

Related pages

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Standard profiles implementation' space='']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:layout><ac:layout-section ac:type="two_right_sidebar"><ac:layout-cell><p>Instead of using low level <em>com.nomagic.uml2.ext.jmi.helpers.StereotypesHelper</em> API to access information about profile elements, it is possible to generate a profile implementation class out of the UML Profile model. This implementation class wraps all mapping, naming details with a nice Java API. MagicDraw comes with already generated profiles implementation for standard profiles. See <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Standard profiles implementation" /></ac:link> page for details.</p><p><br /></p><p>To create your own custom profile implementation you need to follow these steps</p><ol><li>Install Development Tools plugin from Help-&gt;Resource Manager</li><li>Open UML Profile model with MagicDraw. </li><li>Generate profile implementation code using Tools-&gt;Development Tools-&gt;Generate Profile Class Implementation (wrappers) ...</li></ol><p><br /></p><p>Profile implementation class has these features</p><ul><li>There is a constant defined for every DataType, Enumeration name</li><li>There is a getter for every DataType element defined in the profile</li><li>Java enumeration is generated for every Enumeration element defined in the profile. This class has following features<ul><li>There are methods to get enumeration literals by generated enumeration and vice versa</li></ul></li><li>Inner java class is generated for every Stereotype defined in the profile. This class has following features<br /><ul><li>There is a constant defined for Stereotype name</li><li>There are constants defined for all stereotype tags names</li><li>There is a setter/getter method available for every stereotype tag. Signature of these methods corresponds to tag multiplicity type and etc. No more need to work with strings and casting values to primitives as in <em>StereotypesHelper</em> case.</li><li>There is a check method to check if Element has specific stereotype applied</li><li>There is a getter for Stereotype element itself</li></ul></li></ul><p><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="5dce80e5-9c6e-41b1-b514-60eaa771f2dc"><ac:rich-text-body><p>It is highly recommended to assign URI to Profile element in the UML model before generation. URI is an unique Profile identifier and helps to avoid conflicts if model has several profiles with same name applied.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><br /></p><p><strong>Custom sample profile</strong></p><p><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="image2015-7-1 11:26:9.png" /></ac:image></p><p><br /></p><p><strong>Usages example</strong></p><div><p><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="76f1e3c4-f8cd-4155-8c47-5be8016f1e78"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">java</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[Element element = ...;
CustomProfile customProfile = CustomProfile.getInstance(element);
//check if element has Person stereotype applied
customProfile.person().is(element);
//get Person.country tag value
String country = customProfile.person().getCountry(element);
//set Person.country tag value
customProfile.person().setCountry(element, "US");]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><br /></p><pre><strong>Generated implementation class for this custom profile</strong></pre><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="81a5c4b5-6d3a-4665-84b9-2944d60941d9"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">java</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[import com.nomagic.magicdraw.uml.BaseElement;
import com.nomagic.magicdraw.uml2.Profiles;
import com.nomagic.profiles.ProfileCache;
import com.nomagic.profiles.ProfileImplementation;
import com.nomagic.profiles.ProfilesBridge;
import com.nomagic.uml2.ext.magicdraw.classes.mdkernel.Element;
import com.nomagic.uml2.ext.magicdraw.classes.mdkernel.Property;
import com.nomagic.uml2.ext.magicdraw.mdprofiles.Stereotype;

import javax.annotation.CheckForNull;
import java.util.ArrayList;
import java.util.Collection;
import java.util.Collections;
import java.util.HashSet;

@SuppressWarnings("WeakerAccess, unused")
public class CustomProfile extends ProfileImplementation
{
   public static final String PROFILE_URI = "http://custom.com/custom_profile";

   public static final String PROFILE_NAME = "CustomProfile";

   private final PersonStereotype personStereotype;

   public static CustomProfile getInstance(BaseElement baseElement)
   {
      CustomProfile profile = ProfilesBridge.getProfile(CustomProfile.class, baseElement);
      if (profile == null)
      {
         return ProfilesBridge.createProfile(CustomProfile.class, baseElement, CustomProfile::new, PROFILE_NAME, PROFILE_URI);
      }
      return profile;
   }

   public CustomProfile(ProfileCache cache)
   {
      super(cache);
      personStereotype = new PersonStereotype(this);

   }

   public PersonStereotype person()
   {
      return personStereotype;
   }

   public static class PersonStereotype extends StereotypeWrapper
   {

      //stereotype Person and its tags
      public static final String STEREOTYPE_NAME = "Person";
      public static final String COUNTRY = "country";
      public static final String FRIENDS = "friends";

      private final CustomProfile _p;
      @CheckForNull
      private Property country;
      @CheckForNull
      private Property friends;

      protected PersonStereotype(CustomProfile profile)
      {
         super(profile);
         _p = profile;
      }

      @Override
      @SuppressWarnings("ConstantConditions")
      public Stereotype getStereotype()
      {
         return getElementByName(STEREOTYPE_NAME);
      }

      @CheckForNull
      public Property getCountryProperty()
      {
         if (country == null)
         {
            country = getTagByName(getStereotype(), COUNTRY);
         }
         return country;
      }

      @CheckForNull
      public Property getFriendsProperty()
      {
         if (friends == null)
         {
            friends = getTagByName(getStereotype(), FRIENDS);
         }
         return friends;
      }

      public void setCountry(Element element, @CheckForNull String value)
      {
         Profiles.setValue(element, getStereotype(), getCountryProperty(), value);
      }

      public void clearCountry(Element element)
      {
         Profiles.clearValue(element, getCountryProperty());
      }

      @CheckForNull
      public String getCountry(Element element)
      {
         return toString(Profiles.getFirstValue(element, getCountryProperty()));
      }

      public void setFriends(Element element, @CheckForNull java.util.Collection<String> value)
      {
         Profiles.setValue(element, getStereotype(), getFriendsProperty(), value);
      }

      public void clearFriends(Element element)
      {
         Profiles.clearValue(element, getFriendsProperty());
      }

      public void addFriends(Element element, String value)
      {
         Profiles.addValue(element, getStereotype(), getFriendsProperty(), value);
      }

      public void removeFriends(Element element, String value)
      {
         java.util.List<String> values = getFriends(element);
         values.remove(value);
         setFriends(element, values);
      }

      @SuppressWarnings("unchecked")
      public java.util.List<String> getFriends(Element element)
      {
         return (java.util.List<String>) Profiles.getValue(element, getFriendsProperty());
      }

      @Override
      protected void clear()
      {
         super.clear();
         country = null;
         friends = null;
      }

      @Override
      public boolean is(@CheckForNull Element element)
      {
         return element instanceof com.nomagic.uml2.ext.magicdraw.mdusecases.Actor &&
               _p.isTypeOf(element, getStereotype());
      }

      public static boolean isInstance(@CheckForNull Element element)
      {
         if (element instanceof com.nomagic.uml2.ext.magicdraw.mdusecases.Actor)
         {
            CustomProfile instance = getInstance(element);
            return instance.isTypeOf(element, instance.person().getStereotype());
         }
         return false;
      }
   }

   @Override
   protected Collection<ProfileElementWrapper> generatedGetAllElementWrappers()
   {
      Collection<ProfileElementWrapper> wrappers = new ArrayList<>();
      wrappers.add(personStereotype);
      return wrappers;
   }

   @Override
   protected Collection<Stereotype> generatedGetAllStereotypes()
   {
      if (getProfile() != null)
      {
         final Collection<Stereotype> stereotypes = new HashSet<>();
         stereotypes.add(personStereotype.getStereotype());
         return stereotypes;
      }
      return Collections.emptyList();
   }
}]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></div></ac:layout-cell><ac:layout-cell><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="panel" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="8c183a4e-7197-4e37-ba4d-c9762ce8158c"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Related pages</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Standard profiles implementation" /></ac:link></li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section></ac:layout>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=136715226 space=MD2024x version=2 -->
## PAGE 00371: Custom renderer provider

- page_id: `136715226`
- space_key: `MD2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024x/pages/136715226/Custom+renderer+provider
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2024-02-13T08:47:15.122+01:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > Developer Guide > Working with diagrams > Presentation elements (symbols) > Custom presentation elements rendering
- labels: ['presentation-element-render-provider', 'presentation-element-renderer-manager']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

A *com.nomagic.magicdraw.uml.symbols.PresentationElementRendererProvider* is an object, which provides a custom symbol renderer for a given presentation element.

*PresentationElementRendererProvider.getRenderer(**PresentationElement)* method provides a specific renderer for the given *com.nomagic.magicdraw.uml.symbols.PresentationElement* (Symbol) or null.

To make your provider start working, you must register it into the *com.nomagic.magicdraw.uml.symbols.PresentationElementRendererManager*using:

```java

```

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:layout><ac:layout-section ac:type="two_right_sidebar"><ac:layout-cell><p>A <em>com.nomagic.magicdraw.uml.symbols.PresentationElementRendererProvider</em> is an object, which provides a custom symbol renderer for a given presentation element. </p><p><em>PresentationElementRendererProvider.getRenderer(</em><em>PresentationElement)</em> method provides a specific renderer for the given <em>com.nomagic.magicdraw.uml.symbols.PresentationElement</em> (Symbol) or null.</p><p>To make your provider start working, you must register it into the <em>com.nomagic.magicdraw.uml.symbols.PresentationElementRendererManager </em>using:</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="e41b4cdd-300e-44b8-83fc-143bc79ebb89"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">java</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[PresentationElementRendererManager.getInstance().addProvider(new RendererProvider());]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></ac:layout-cell><ac:layout-cell><p><br /></p></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section></ac:layout>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=136715231 space=MD2024x version=1 -->
## PAGE 00372: Custom renderers sample

- page_id: `136715231`
- space_key: `MD2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024x/pages/136715231/Custom+renderers+sample
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-09-20T18:17:14.410+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > Developer Guide > Working with diagrams > Presentation elements (symbols) > Custom presentation elements rendering
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

This example covers:

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Custom renderers sample' space='MD2024x'] The package without inner elements is filled with a green color.
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Custom renderers sample' space='MD2024x'] The slot values are rounded.
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Custom renderers sample' space='MD2024x'] The dependency link is a blue thicker line with custom line ends.

```text

```

This example shows, how to create custom renderers for the preceding pointed symbols.

```text

```

**The custom package rendererThe custom package renderer.**The empty package (that is without inner elements) is filled with green color:

```java

```

**The custom slot rendererThe custom slot renderer (used in the instance specification symbol).**The slot values are rounded:

```java
values = slot.getValue();
                        for (ValueSpecification value : values)
                        {
                            if (value instanceof LiteralString)
                            {
                                LiteralString literalString = (LiteralString) value;
                                if (string.length() > 0)
                                {
                                   string += "; ";

                                }
                                String literalValue = literalString.getValue();
                                try
                                {
                                    // round a value
                                    double doubleValue = Double.parseDouble(literalValue); 
                                    double rounded = Math.round(doubleValue * 100) / 100; 
                                    literalValue = Double.toString(rounded);
                                }
                                catch (NumberFormatException e)
                                {
                                }
                                string += literalValue;
                            }
                        }
                        return slot.getDefiningFeature().getName() + "=" + string;
                    }
                }
            }
            return super.getText(presentationElement, textEnum);
        }
    }]]>
```

**The custom dependency link rendererThe custom dependency link renderer.**The dependency link is a blue thicker line with custom line ends:

```java

```

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:layout><ac:layout-section ac:type="two_right_sidebar"><ac:layout-cell><p>This example covers:</p><ul><li style="text-align: left;"><strong style="line-height: 1.4285715;"><ac:link ac:anchor="The custom package renderer"><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[A custom renderer for the package.]]></ac:plain-text-link-body><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Custom renderers sample" /></ac:link> </strong>The package without inner elements is filled with a green color.</li><li style="text-align: left;"><strong style="line-height: 1.4285715;"><ac:link ac:anchor="The custom slot renderer"><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[A custom renderer for the slot.]]></ac:plain-text-link-body><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Custom renderers sample" /></ac:link> </strong>The slot values are rounded.</li><li style="text-align: left;"><strong style="line-height: 1.4285715;"><ac:link ac:anchor="The custom dependency link renderer"><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[A custom renderer for the dependency link.]]></ac:plain-text-link-body><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Custom renderers sample" /></ac:link> </strong>The dependency link is a blue thicker line with custom line ends.</li></ul><div class="WordSection8"><pre> </pre><p>This example shows, how to create custom renderers for the preceding pointed symbols.</p><pre><strong style="line-height: 1.4285715;"><br /></strong></pre><p><strong style="line-height: 1.4285715;"><ac:structured-macro ac:name="anchor" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="397ce5c4-bcbc-4d41-95af-cef715ca7ce5"><ac:parameter ac:name="">The custom package renderer</ac:parameter></ac:structured-macro>The custom package renderer. </strong>The empty package (that is without inner elements) is filled with green color:</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="be4a4052-0b22-4a8d-82d9-666a84f7689c"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">java</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[    class PackageRenderer extends ShapeRenderer
    {
        public Color getColor(PresentationElement presentationElement, PresentationElementColorEnum colorEnum)
        {
            if (PresentationElementColorEnum.FILL.equals(colorEnum))
            {
                // the color to fill
                Element element = presentationElement.getElement();
                if (element instanceof com.nomagic.uml2.ext.magicdraw.classes.mdkernel.Package && 
                    !((com.nomagic.uml2.ext.magicdraw.classes.mdkernel.Package) element).hasOwnedElement())
                {
                    // the package has no elements, use the green color to fill 
                    return Color.GREEN;
                }
            }
            return super.getColor(presentationElement, colorEnum);
        }
    }]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p> </p><p><strong><ac:structured-macro ac:name="anchor" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="ad861a70-cfba-48c7-8f42-1e2ef552c0df"><ac:parameter ac:name="">The custom slot renderer</ac:parameter></ac:structured-macro>The custom slot renderer (used in the instance specification symbol). </strong>The slot values are rounded:</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="b629cb6d-b54a-4f8e-b68a-2e1114e57727"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">java</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[    class SlotRenderer extends ShapeRenderer
    {
        public String getText(PresentationElement presentationElement, PresentationElementTextEnum textEnum)
        {
            if (PresentationElementTextEnum.NAME.equals(textEnum))
            {
                // the slot text is shown as a name
                Element element = presentationElement.getElement();
                if (element instanceof Slot)
                {
                    Slot slot = (Slot) element;
                    if (slot.hasValue())
                    {
                        String string = "";
                        List<ValueSpecification> values = slot.getValue();
                        for (ValueSpecification value : values)
                        {
                            if (value instanceof LiteralString)
                            {
                                LiteralString literalString = (LiteralString) value;
                                if (string.length() > 0)
                                {
                                   string += "; ";

                                }
                                String literalValue = literalString.getValue();
                                try
                                {
                                    // round a value
                                    double doubleValue = Double.parseDouble(literalValue); 
                                    double rounded = Math.round(doubleValue * 100) / 100; 
                                    literalValue = Double.toString(rounded);
                                }
                                catch (NumberFormatException e)
                                {
                                }
                                string += literalValue;
                            }
                        }
                        return slot.getDefiningFeature().getName() + "=" + string;
                    }
                }
            }
            return super.getText(presentationElement, textEnum);
        }
    }]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p> </p><p><strong><ac:structured-macro ac:name="anchor" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="09575b30-840e-4689-aeec-e5d212c2e471"><ac:parameter ac:name="">The custom dependency link renderer</ac:parameter></ac:structured-macro>The custom dependency link renderer. </strong>The dependency link is a blue thicker line with custom line ends:</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="817c14d2-f29c-4c12-a37b-0098e2d0b912"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">java</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[    class DependencyRenderer extends PathRenderer
    {
        private PathEndRenderer mClientEndRenderer; 
        DependencyRenderer()
        {
            // a custom client end renderer, use a filled circle at the end
            mClientEndRenderer = new PathEndRenderer(PathEndAdornment.CIRCLE, PathEndAdornmentModifier.FILLED);
        }
 
        public Color getColor(PresentationElement presentationElement, PresentationElementColorEnum colorEnum)
        {
            if (PresentationElementColorEnum.LINE.equals(colorEnum))
            {
                // use a blue color for a line 
                return Color.BLUE;
            }
            return super.getColor(presentationElement, colorEnum);
        }


        protected PathEndRenderer getClientEndRenderer(PathElement pathElement)
        {
            // use a custom end renderer 
            return mClientEndRenderer;
        }
 
        public int getLineWidth(PresentationElement presentationElement)
        {
            // a line width is 2 
            return 2;
        }
 
        protected void drawPathAdornment(Graphics g, PathElement pathElement)
        {
            super.drawPathAdornment(g, pathElement);
 
            // draw a circle at the middle of the dependency line
            Color background = Color.WHITE;
            Property property = pathElement.getDiagramPresentationElement().getProperty(PropertyID.DIAGRAM_BACKGROUND_COLOR);
            if (property != null)
            {
                Object value = property.getValue();
                if (value instanceof Color)
                {
                    background = (Color) value;
                }
            }
            Point middlePoint = pathElement.getMiddlePoint();
            int diameter = 10;
            int radius = diameter / 2;
            int x = middlePoint.x - radius;
            int y = middlePoint.y - radius;
            Color color = g.getColor(); 
            g.setColor(background);
            g.fillOval(x, y, diameter, diameter);
            g.setColor(color);
            g.drawOval(x, y, diameter, diameter);
        }
    }]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro></div></ac:layout-cell><ac:layout-cell><p> </p></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section></ac:layout>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=136714605 space=MD2024x version=1 -->
## PAGE 00374: Custom tool

- page_id: `136714605`
- space_key: `MD2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024x/pages/136714605/Custom+tool
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-09-20T18:16:42.946+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > Report Wizard > Appendix A: report extensions
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

A Custom tool is a template library written in Java to encapsulate functions in context. The Custom tool, see figure below, is extended from the report API. The tool can be used to develop custom context properties that are presentation centric or that can take advantage of the existing ‘tool’ extensions or by document authors familiar with Java.

Report Wizard uses the word 'tool' meaning an object encapsulating dynamic functions on the JavaBean. A tool is a "carrier" of data between the Java and template layers. In other words, the tool simplifies template development and maintenance.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Custom tool API Structure.

#### Context Name

A Context name is a name for variables or references in the template. Report Wizard uses the Velocity Template Language (*VTL*) as a standard syntax for the template. *VTL* uses “$” as a leading character followed by an identifier for the variable.

For example:

```text

```

#### Context Object

A Context object is a Java Object representing a variable. A Context object is modeled on the JavaBean specifications defined by Sun Microsystems.

For example, if a String represents the variable $foo.name in context, the value of the String will be printed out in the output report as follows.

```text

```

Report Wizard uses the Velocity Template Language (<em>VTL</em>) as a standard syntax for the template. <em>VTL</em> uses “$” as a leading character followed by an identifier for the variable.</p><p>For example:</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="beb915e6-b6b2-4bfa-984e-1ff86fe8051f"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[$UseCase

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=136712265 space=MD2024x version=1 -->
## PAGE 00375: Custom traceability rules

- page_id: `136712265`
- space_key: `MD2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024x/pages/136712265/Custom+traceability+rules
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-09-20T18:14:22.906+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > Model analysis > Traceability
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

#### CONTENT-LAYER: Editing traceability rule values

1966009052

#### CONTENT-COLUMN: Editing traceability rule values

1966009054

1974237355

**On this page**

4

#### CONTENT-BLOCK: Editing traceability rule values

1966009053

You can create custom traceability rules if predefined traceability rules are not sufficient to trace and analyze relationships between elements in your model. In a model, custom traceability rules are considered as [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Extending Metamodel with Derived Properties' space='MD2024x'], because their values are produced or calculated from other properties. Usually, the values of custom traceability rules cannot be changed directly, but you can [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Custom traceability rules' space='MD2024x'] and [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Custom traceability rules' space='MD2024x'] these rules the same way as any other property.

##### Creating a custom traceability rule

Custom traceability rules can be quickly created from the Specification window of a particular element. This way you create a new Derived Property for all elements of the selected element type.

The customization of the newly created traceability rule is saved in the *Derived Properties* Package with the «derivedPropertiesSuite» stereotype. This folder is normally hidden in the containment tree of your model. You can find it using the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Quick Find dialog' space='MD2024x']**Quick Find** functionality. This may be useful when you want to change traceability rule properties.

To create a custom traceability rule

1. In the model browser or a diagram, double-click the element for which you want to create a custom traceability rule to open its Specification window.
2. In the property group list on the left side of the Specification window, select the Traceability group.
3. Click the Create button. [ATTACHMENT filename='1_create_custom_traceability_rule_1.png']
4. In the open Expression dialog, specify the name and [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Specifying criteria for querying model' space='MD2024x'] of a new traceability rule.
5. Click OK and close the Specification window. If you want to create a Derived Property with a result other than an element, you need to change the Derived Property type manually. [ATTACHMENT filename='1_create_custom_traceability_rule_2.png']

To create a custom traceability rule in a table

- In the toolbar of an active table, click the Columns button, and in the menu select the New Derived Property command. [ATTACHMENT filename='new_derived_property_in_a_table.png']

##### Specifying a custom traceability rule

When you create a new custom traceability rule for an element or a Derived Property in a table, you need to define the expression in the **Expression** dialog. Keep in mind:

- The Derived Property type and multiplicity (conveyed via the Single Value check box) are set automatically according to the specified expression once it is created or edited. You can also change them manually, however, they will only be applied if you make no modifications in the expression itself. [ATTACHMENT filename='derived_property_type_and_multiplicity_img.png']
- The root union of a Derived Property structured expression has the Unique check box which you can select to have only one identical element count as the result. By default, the check box is selected. [ATTACHMENT filename='unique_ordered_options.png']

#### INFO: Additional information

Additional information

To learn more about specifying expressions, see the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Specifying criteria for querying model' space='MD2024x'] page.

##### editEditing a custom traceability rule

You can modify a custom traceability rule from the Specification window of a particular element.

#### NOTE: Editing traceability rule values

Editing traceability rule values

You can edit the value of a custom traceability rule directly only when its operation type is [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Using Simple Navigation' space='MD2024x'] and you specified only one criterion.

To edit a custom traceability rule

1. In the model browser or a diagram, double-click the element with the traceability rule you want to edit to open its Specification window.
2. In the property group list on the left side of the Specification window, select the Traceability group.
3. In the property specification area on the right side of the window, select the traceability rule you want to edit.
4. Click the Edit button. [ATTACHMENT filename='2_edit_custom_traceability_rule_1.png']
5. In the Expression dialog, make desired changes and click OK .
6. Close the Specification window.

##### editEditing a custom traceability rule in a table

If a custom traceability rule is displayed in a table, you can edit it directly from that table.

To edit a custom traceability rule in a table

1. In a table, right-click the header of the desired custom traceability rule column and select Edit Derived Property . [ATTACHMENT filename='2_edit_custom_traceability_rule_in_table.png']
2. In the Expression dialog, make desired changes and click OK .

##### displayDisplaying a custom traceability rule

If you want to visualize custom traceability rules, you can display them in the compartments of element shapes or in tables.

To display a custom traceability rule on an element shape

1. Do one of the following:
  - In a diagram, right-click the desired element shape and select Edit Compartments .
  - In a diagram, select the desired element shape and click the **Compartments** smart manipulator, click on the down arrow to expand the menu, and select **Edit Compartments**.
2. In the **Compartment Edit** dialog, select the **Element Properties** tab.
3. In the Hidden list on the left side of the dialog, select the traceability rules you want to display.
4. Click [ATTACHMENT filename='add_to_selected.png'] to add the selected traceability rules to the Selected list on the right side of the dialog. If you want to display all properties from the Hidden list, click [ATTACHMENT filename='add_all.png'] .
5. Click OK . [ATTACHMENT filename='3_display_custom_traceability_rule_on_element_shape.png']

To display a custom traceability rule in a table

1. In the toolbar of an active table, click the Show Columns button and from the menu select the Select Columns command. [ATTACHMENT filename='3_display_custom_traceability_rule_on_in_table_1.png']
2. In the Select Columns dialog, set the traceability rules you want to display as true .
3. Click OK . [ATTACHMENT filename='3_display_custom_traceability_rule_on_in_table_2.png']

##### Removing a custom traceability rule

You can remove a Custom traceability rule from the Specification window of a particular element or directly from a table if the traceability rule is displayed in it.

To remove a custom traceability rule

1. In the model browser or a diagram, double-click the element with the traceability rule you want to remove to open its Specification window.
2. In the property group list on the left side of the Specification window, select the Traceability group.
3. In the property specification area on the right side of the window, select the traceability rule you want to remove.
4. Click the Delete button and close the Specification window. [ATTACHMENT filename='4_remove_custom_traceability_rule.png']

To remove a custom traceability rule in a table

- In a table, right-click the header of the unwanted custom traceability rule column and select Remove Derived Property . [ATTACHMENT filename='4_remove_custom_traceability_rule_in_table.png']

1966009050

**Related pages**

- Traceability
  - Creating traceability relations
  - Traceability relations representation
  - Navigating between different levels of abstraction
  - Analyzing traceability relations
  - Predefined traceability rules

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-layer" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="954cb7b3-1674-428c-9093-6ccf4523cf63"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1966009052</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-column" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="9ba59a78-c5ad-4fd0-a8a7-87f39b3337da"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1966009054</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="e9893ca7-1850-47d8-944a-38b5e567a1ea"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1974237355</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>On this page</strong></p><p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="toc" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="ee6dfe0d-33e0-4e96-8e8a-6fbae61e8e0a"><ac:parameter ac:name="maxLevel">4</ac:parameter></ac:structured-macro></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="2d3c20af-bb3c-43f8-bcc3-35546f1e561d"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1966009053</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>You can create custom traceability rules if predefined traceability rules are not sufficient to trace and analyze relationships between elements in your model. In a model, custom traceability rules are considered as <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Extending Metamodel with Derived Properties" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Derived Properties]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>, because their values are produced or calculated from other properties. Usually, the values of custom traceability rules cannot be changed directly, but you can <ac:link ac:anchor="edit"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Custom traceability rules" /></ac:link> and <ac:link ac:anchor="display"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Custom traceability rules" /></ac:link> these rules the same way as any other property.</p><h3>Creating a custom traceability rule</h3><p>Custom traceability rules can be quickly created from the Specification window of a particular element. This way you create a new Derived Property for all elements of the selected element type.</p><p>The customization of the newly created traceability rule is saved in the <em>Derived Properties</em> Package with the «derivedPropertiesSuite» stereotype. This folder is normally hidden in the containment tree of your model. You can find it using the <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Quick Find dialog" /><ac:link-body><strong>Quick Find</strong> functionality</ac:link-body></ac:link>. This may be useful when you want to change traceability rule properties.</p><p><br /></p><p>To create a custom traceability rule</p><hr /><ol><li>In the model browser or a diagram, double-click the element for which you want to create a custom traceability rule to open its Specification window.</li><li>In the property group list on the left side of the Specification window, select the <strong>Traceability</strong> group.</li><li>Click the <strong>Create</strong> button.<br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="1_create_custom_traceability_rule_1.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Custom traceability rules" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></li><li>In the open <strong>Expression</strong> dialog, specify the name and <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Specifying criteria for querying model" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[expression]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> of a new traceability rule.</li><li>Click <strong>OK</strong> and close the Specification window. If you want to create a Derived Property with a result other than an element, you need to change the Derived Property type manually.<br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="1_create_custom_traceability_rule_2.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Custom traceability rules" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></li></ol><p><br /></p><p>To create a custom traceability rule in a table</p><hr /><ul><li>In the toolbar of an active table, click the <strong style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">Columns</strong> button, and in the menu select the <strong style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">New Derived Property</strong> command.<br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="new_derived_property_in_a_table.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Custom traceability rules" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></li></ul><h3>Specifying a custom traceability rule</h3><p>When you create a new custom traceability rule for an element or a Derived Property in a table, you need to define the expression in the <strong>Expression</strong> dialog. Keep in mind:</p><ul><li>The Derived Property <strong>type</strong> and <strong>multiplicity</strong> (conveyed via the <strong>Single Value</strong> check box) are set automatically according to the specified expression once it is created or edited. You can also change them manually, however, they will only be applied if you make no modifications in the expression itself.<br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="derived_property_type_and_multiplicity_img.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Custom traceability rules" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></li><li>The root union of a Derived Property structured expression has the <strong>Unique</strong> check box which you can select to have only one identical element count as the result. By default, the check box is selected.<br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="unique_ordered_options.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Custom traceability rules" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></li></ul><ac:structured-macro ac:name="info" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="62275b61-1db5-4461-b210-61eb3e15d88c"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Additional information</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>To learn more about specifying expressions, see the <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Specifying criteria for querying model" /></ac:link> page.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><h3><ac:structured-macro ac:name="anchor" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="93bf5c32-88d1-4c06-8c19-7f19c09e08af"><ac:parameter ac:name="">edit</ac:parameter></ac:structured-macro>Editing a custom traceability rule</h3><p>You can modify a custom traceability rule from the Specification window of a particular element.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="0b289255-dbc9-4181-bf70-f88863e32b1c"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Editing traceability rule values</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>You can edit the value of a custom traceability rule directly only when its operation type is <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Using Simple Navigation" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Simple Navigation]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> and you specified only one criterion.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><br /></p><p>To edit a custom traceability rule</p><hr /><ol><li>In the model browser or a diagram, double-click the element with the traceability rule you want to edit to open its Specification window.</li><li>In the property group list on the left side of the Specification window, select the <strong>Traceability</strong> group.</li><li>In the property specification area on the right side of the window, select the traceability rule you want to edit.</li><li>Click the <strong>Edit</strong> button.<br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="2_edit_custom_traceability_rule_1.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Custom traceability rules" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></li><li>In the <strong>Expression</strong> dialog, make desired changes and click <strong>OK</strong>.</li><li>Close the Specification window.</li></ol><h3><ac:structured-macro ac:name="anchor" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="606f87d0-f0a8-4715-a9b8-5a0beea6b569"><ac:parameter ac:name="">edit</ac:parameter></ac:structured-macro>Editing a custom traceability rule in a table</h3><p><span>If a custom traceability rule is displayed in a table, you can edit it directly from that table.<br /><br /></span></p><p>To edit a custom traceability rule in a table</p><hr /><ol><li>In a table, right-click the header of the desired custom traceability rule column and select <strong>Edit Derived Property</strong>.<br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="2_edit_custom_traceability_rule_in_table.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Custom traceability rules" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></li><li>In the <strong>Expression</strong> dialog, make desired changes and click <strong>OK</strong>.</li></ol><h3><ac:structured-macro ac:name="anchor" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="0874203b-e27b-4e7d-ad41-efcd9d0dde9e"><ac:parameter ac:name="">display</ac:parameter></ac:structured-macro>Displaying a custom traceability rule</h3><p>If you want to visualize custom traceability rules, you can display them in the compartments of element shapes or in tables.</p><p><br /></p><p>To display a custom traceability rule on an element shape</p><hr /><ol><li>Do one of the following:<br /><ul><li>In a diagram, right-click the desired element shape and select <strong>Edit Compartments</strong>.</li><li><p>In a diagram, select the desired element shape and click the <strong>Compartments</strong> smart manipulator, click on the down arrow to expand the menu, and select <strong>Edit Compartments</strong>.</p></li></ul></li><li><p>In the <strong>Compartment Edit</strong> dialog, select the <strong>Element Properties</strong> tab.</p></li><li>In the <strong>Hidden</strong> list on the left side of the dialog, select the traceability rules you want to display.</li><li>Click <ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="add_to_selected.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="_buttons" /></ri:attachment></ac:image> to add the selected traceability rules to the <strong>Selected</strong> list on the right side of the dialog. If you want to display all properties from the <strong>Hidden</strong> list, click <ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="add_all.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="_buttons" /></ri:attachment></ac:image>.</li><li>Click <strong>OK</strong>.<br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="3_display_custom_traceability_rule_on_element_shape.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Custom traceability rules" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></li></ol><p><br /></p><p>To display a custom traceability rule in a table</p><hr /><ol><li>In the toolbar of an active table, click the <strong>Show Columns</strong> button and from the menu select the <strong>Select Columns</strong> command.<br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="3_display_custom_traceability_rule_on_in_table_1.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Custom traceability rules" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></li><li>In the <strong>Select Columns</strong> dialog, set the traceability rules you want to display as <em>true</em>.</li><li>Click <strong>OK</strong>.<br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="3_display_custom_traceability_rule_on_in_table_2.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Custom traceability rules" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></li></ol><h3><br />Removing a custom traceability rule</h3><p>You can remove a Custom traceability rule from the Specification window of a particular element or directly from a table if the traceability rule is displayed in it.</p><p><br /></p><p>To remove a custom traceability rule</p><hr /><ol><li>In the model browser or a diagram, double-click the element with the traceability rule you want to remove to open its Specification window.</li><li>In the property group list on the left side of the Specification window, select the <strong>Traceability</strong> group.</li><li>In the property specification area on the right side of the window, select the traceability rule you want to remove.</li><li>Click the <strong>Delete</strong> button and close the Specification window.<br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="4_remove_custom_traceability_rule.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Custom traceability rules" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></li></ol><p><br /></p><p>To remove a custom traceability rule in a table</p><hr /><ul><li>In a table, right-click the header of the unwanted custom traceability rule column and select <strong>Remove Derived Property</strong>.<br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="4_remove_custom_traceability_rule_in_table.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Custom traceability rules" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="9a15363d-3bab-4c1e-b912-4b2bb1b611c9"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1966009050</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">Related pages</strong></p><ul><li class="ancestor-link parent-link"><a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MD2024x/Traceability">Traceability</a><ul><li><a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MD2024x/Creating+traceability+relations">Creating traceability relations</a></li><li><a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MD2024x/Traceability+relations+representation">Traceability relations representation</a></li><li><a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MD2024x/Navigating+between+different+levels+of+abstraction">Navigating between different levels of abstraction</a></li><li><a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MD2024x/Analyzing+traceability+relations">Analyzing traceability relations</a></li><li><a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MD2024x/Predefined+traceability+rules">Predefined traceability rules</a></li></ul></li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=136714931 space=MD2024x version=1 -->
## PAGE 00377: Customization Element Properties

- page_id: `136714931`
- space_key: `MD2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024x/pages/136714931/Customization+Element+Properties
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-09-20T18:16:57.632+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > UML Profiling and DSL Guide > Domain Specific Language customization > Creating Customization Data
- labels: ['attribute', 'customiztion-attribute', 'element-attribute', 'metaproperty', 'meta-property']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

#### CONTENT-LAYER: Properties

1578480678

#### CONTENT-COLUMN: Properties

1578480680

#### CONTENT-BLOCK: Properties

1578480679

#### NOTE: Properties

Properties

There are two property meanings used in customization elements: properties specifying the element (in the[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Specification window' space='MD2024x']), and properties as element attributes. The latter is described in this section.

You can create a property in the customization element, then apply the particular stereotype and create customization rules.

To create a property in the customization element

1. In a [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Profile diagram' space='MD2024x'] , create a customization element.
2. Select the customization element on the diagram.
3. Click the [ATTACHMENT filename='add_property.png'] button. The menu with properties appears. [ATTACHMENT filename='Create_Element_Menu.png']
4. Select the desired property described in the following table:

| Attribute | Applied Stereotype | Description |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Place on Diagram Palette | «placeOnPaletteProperty» |  |
| Meta Property | «metaProperty» | Changes the name of the default meta property. |
| Property Group | «propertyGroup» | Creates your own groups and subgroups to group the properties either of standard UML metaclasses or stereotypes. |
| Auto Numbering Property | «AutoNumber» | Creates property used to specify numbering rules for a DSL element. |
| Derived Property Specification | «derivedPropertySpecification» | Creates a derived property that is the property whose values are calculated automatically from the other properties’ values. |

1578480677

**Related pages:**

- Creating Customization Data
- Using Customization Data

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE
