<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=136709604 space=MD2024x version=2 -->
## PAGE 00408: Decomposing model

- page_id: `136709604`
- space_key: `MD2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024x/pages/136709604/Decomposing+model
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2024-01-30T15:03:19.171+01:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > Working with projects > Project partitioning
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

305246055

305246057

305246056

Model decomposition has a package level granularity. Smaller elements cannot be split into separate projects. Basically, each project package could be partitioned into separate projects; however, this is excessive.

The decision on how to split a model into parts should be made carefully. You should isolate model parts that form logically complete pieces of a structure (e.g., a subsystem, code library, or profile) and are not very dependent on each other.

When there are many one-way dependencies to a model part (e.g., parts **A**, **B**, **C** depend on a part **D**, but part **D** does not depend on any of the parts **A**, **B**, **C**), this part is a good candidate for placement into a separate project.

When one big project is used to store all modeling information (e.g., use case models, high level architectural models, detailed implementation level class, sequence, state, and other information), it may be useful to partition the models according to the modeling domains (i.e., use cases in one used project, architectural models in another, implementation level models in yet another). This allows unloading unnecessary used projects while working on one part (and improving the performance), while retaining the relationships between domains and loading used projects on demand.

Avoid decomposing a model into parts which have circular dependencies. That is, A ↔ B or A → B → C → A situations.

Usually programmers are very adept at splitting large code bases into libraries. The very same criteria should be applied for splitting large models into separate projects.

The MagicDraw project decomposition functionality allows two important options:

- Working without all used projects loaded.
- Using the project in the read-write accessibility mode.

Used projects are often exploited for storing profiles. However, a used project is not a profile, and it is important not to mix them. Any model part can be stored in a separate project.

305246054

**Related pages**

****

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

Smaller elements cannot be split into separate projects. Basically, each project package could be partitioned into separate projects; however, this is excessive.</p><p>The decision on how to split a model into parts should be made carefully. You should isolate model parts that form logically complete pieces of a structure (e.g., a subsystem, code library, or profile) and are not very dependent on each other.</p><p>When there are many one-way dependencies to a model part (e.g., parts <strong>A</strong>, <strong>B</strong>, <strong>C</strong> depend on a part <strong>D</strong>, but part <strong>D</strong> does not depend on any of the parts <strong>A</strong>, <strong>B</strong>, <strong>C</strong>), this part is a good candidate for placement into a separate project.</p><p>When one big project is used to store all modeling information (e.g., use case models, high level architectural models, detailed implementation level class, sequence, state, and other information), it may be useful to partition the models according to the modeling domains (i.e., use cases in one used project, architectural models in another, implementation level models in yet another). This allows unloading unnecessary used projects while working on one part (and improving the performance), while retaining the relationships between domains and loading used projects on demand.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="b77ac731-7a31-4bfe-9436-2348fb7622de"><ac:rich-text-body><p>Avoid decomposing a model into parts which have circular dependencies. That is, A ↔ B or A → B <span>→</span> C <span>→</span> A situations.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p>Usually programmers are very adept at splitting large code bases into libraries. The very same criteria should be applied for splitting large models into separate projects.</p><p>The MagicDraw project decomposition functionality allows two important options:</p><ul><li>Working without all used projects loaded.</li><li>Using the project in the read-write accessibility mode.</li></ul><p>Used projects are often exploited for storing profiles. However, a used project is not a profile, and it is important not to mix them. Any model part can be stored in a separate project.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="ba03300e-159f-4934-aac8-741d975234fa"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">305246054</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Related pages</strong></p><p><strong><ac:structured-macro ac:name="children" ac:schema-version="2" ac:macro-id="c1ede1e2-33f4-4cd9-a7dd-fc9a68bbcb8d" /><br /></strong></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=136711324 space=MD2024x version=1 -->
## PAGE 00409: Deep History

- page_id: `136711324`
- space_key: `MD2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024x/pages/136711324/Deep+History
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-09-20T18:13:32.656+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > UML elements > State Machine > Pseudo States
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

The Deep History represents the most recent active configuration of the composite state that directly contains this pseudo state; e.g. the state configuration that was active when the composite state was last exited. A composite state can have at most one deep history vertex.

#### PANEL: Related Pages

Related Pages

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='UML elements' space='MD2024x']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Specification window' space='MD2024x']

#### PANEL: Related pages

Related pages

[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Pseudo States' space='']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:layout><ac:layout-section ac:type="two_right_sidebar"><ac:layout-cell><p>The Deep History represents the most recent active configuration of the composite state that directly contains this pseudo state; e.g. the state configuration that was active when the composite state was last exited. A composite state can have at most one deep history vertex.</p></ac:layout-cell><ac:layout-cell><p> </p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="panel" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="36e20852-22dd-420b-8d4b-52b136b22981"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Related Pages</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="UML elements" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Model Elements]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Specification window" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Specification Window]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link></li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="panel" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="f01b84d9-d9d1-4049-8315-15a6a05d850d"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Related pages</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="children" ac:schema-version="2" ac:macro-id="7e19d119-2f65-4f2a-b944-60c34f7c4ba8"><ac:parameter ac:name="page"><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Pseudo States" /></ac:link></ac:parameter></ac:structured-macro></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section></ac:layout>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=136711548 space=MD2024x version=1 -->
## PAGE 00410: Default property values

- page_id: `136711548`
- space_key: `MD2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024x/pages/136711548/Default+property+values
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-09-20T18:13:44.485+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > Working with model elements
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

60307427

60307430

60316126

**On this page**

60307429

The modeling tool allows you to define the initial (default) properties for elements.

The Default element properties can be defined for:

- The whole project
- The specific diagram

To set the default properties for the whole project

1. From the Options menu, select Project . The Project Options dialog opens.
2. Expand Default model properties . Select the desired elements and change their property values.

After creating a new element it will have new property values. Values for previously created elements will not be changed.

To reset element properties to the default value

- In he Project Options dialog, Default model properties group, click the Reset to Defaults button.

Exception: interface attribute default visibility will always be #public, no matter what your settings are.

To set default properties for the specific diagram

1. From the Diagrams menu, select Customize . The [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Customizing diagrams' space='MD2024x'] dialog opens.
2. Select the diagram type you want to customize.
3. Click the Edit button. The Customize Diagram Wizard opens.
4. In the fourth step Specify toolbar buttons , click the Add button and select New Button . The Edit Button dialog opens.
5. In the **General** > **Model Element Type** drop-down list, select the element, which default properties you want to customize.
6. In the **Edit Button** dialog, open the **Element Properties** tab.
7. Select the **Specify own values** radio button. Now change the default property values of the chosen element.

Create a new element from the customized diagram toolbar and the element default properties will be the same as you had defined.

##### Sharing the default property values

You can share the default properties with other users for their new project. After changing the property values in the **Project Options** dialog, create a project template.

To create a template

1. From the File menu select Export > Template
2. Enter the template name and description.
3. Click OK . A template will be created in <moleding tool installation directory>\ templates .

To import a template

1. From the File menu, select New Project . The New Project dialog opens.
2. From the Other domain, select the Project from Template icon.
3. In the Select template tree, choose your template. The project options are imported to the project together with the template.

60307425

**Related pages**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Working with model elements' space='MD2024x']

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=136711733 space=MD2024x version=1 -->
## PAGE 00411: Default settings of hyperlinks to model elements

- page_id: `136711733`
- space_key: `MD2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024x/pages/136711733/Default+settings+of+hyperlinks+to+model+elements
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-09-20T18:13:52.435+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > Working with model elements > Manage Navigation > Defining hyperlinks > Hyperlinks in texts
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

61862155

61862158

61862156

When adding hyperlinks in text to refer to model elements, the values of **Text to display box**is **Name** and **Update Mode**is **Automatically updated** are selected by default. You can change the default settings in the **Project Options** dialog.

To change the default settings of a hyperlink to model element

1. From the main menu, select Options > Project .
2. In the **Project Options** dialog , expand the General property group in the tree.
3. Select Element References .
4. Select the option for the value you want to change:
  - Select one of the Default Element Hyperlink Display Mode option values: - **Name**(Default). Displays the element name from its Specification window. 
- **Representation text.** Displays the full element title visible in the Containment tree. 
- **Icon+Representation text*.*** Displays the element icon together with the full title from the Containment tree. 
- **Custom text**. Allows you to type your own text in the text box; however, then you cannot specify the **Update mode** option.
  - Select one of the Default Element Hyperlink Update Mode option values: - Automatically updated (Default) . Updates hyperlink text or the referenced element name automatically. - Automatically checked . Enables active validation rules which check if the hyperlink and the referenced element are coherent. - Manually checked. Allows you to run validation manually to check if the hyperlink and the referenced element are coherent. - Do not update . Does not update if the hyperlink text and the referenced element name become inconsistent. Validation will not find any inconsistencies.
5. Click OK . The default settings of hyperlinks to elements are changed.

61862154

3[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Defining hyperlinks' space='']

- Managing hyperlinks
- Linking model elements to OSLC resources

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=136714074 space=MD2024x version=1 -->
## PAGE 00412: Default templates

- page_id: `136714074`
- space_key: `MD2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024x/pages/136714074/Default+templates
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-09-20T18:16:07.040+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > Report Wizard > Generating Reports from Report Wizard
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

There are 12 default templates that come with the report engine.

1. Class Specification Report describes the specification of class, interface, and enumeration type of the project.
2. Data Dictionary Report enables you to print elements of MagicDraw data dictionary.
3. IEEE 1233 the standard template based on the IEEE 1233 recommendation.
4. Model Extension describes common UML model extension mechanisms (stereotypes, tagged values, and constraints). It is useful for reporting custom UML profiles.
5. Use Case (Simple) describes system functionalities and actors in a simple format.
6. Use Case (Modern) describes system functionalities and actors that are useful in support of use-case driven analysis.
7. Web Publisher (Simple HTML) enables you to publish MagicDraw models (including diagrams) in simple HTML. The report is viewable using a standard browser such as Internet Explorer or Firefox.
8. Web Publisher 2.0 enables you to publish MagicDraw models (including diagrams) in HTML with rich features. The report is viewable using a standard browser such as Internet Explorer or Firefox.
9. Activity Diagram provides a standard report for activity diagrams. The content of the report is a list of all the nodes (ordered by node hierarchy in each activity diagram).
10. Activity Diagram Specification provides a standard report for activities. The content of the report is a list of all the nodes in each activity.
11. Sequence Diagram provides a report that shows sequence diagrams and a list of all the messages in the diagrams.
12. Sequence Diagram Specification provides a report that shows sequence diagrams, and a list of all the messages and lifelines in the diagrams.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html

It is useful for reporting custom UML profiles.</li><li><strong>Use Case (Simple)</strong> <br />describes system functionalities and actors in a simple format.</li><li><strong>Use Case (Modern)</strong> <br />describes system functionalities and actors that are useful in support of use-case driven analysis.</li><li><strong>Web Publisher (Simple HTML)</strong> <br />enables you to publish MagicDraw models (including diagrams) in simple HTML. The report is viewable using a standard browser such as Internet Explorer or Firefox.</li><li><strong>Web Publisher 2.0</strong> <br />enables you to publish MagicDraw models (including diagrams) in HTML with rich features. The report is viewable using a standard browser such as Internet Explorer or Firefox.</li><li><strong>Activity Diagram</strong> <br />provides a standard report for activity diagrams. The content of the report is a list of all the nodes (ordered by node hierarchy in each activity diagram).</li><li><strong>Activity Diagram Specification</strong> <br />provides a standard report for activities. The content of the report is a list of all the nodes in each activity.</li><li><strong>Sequence Diagram</strong> <br />provides a report that shows sequence diagrams and a list of all the messages in the diagrams.</li><li><strong>Sequence Diagram Specification</strong> <br />provides a report that shows sequence diagrams, and a list of all the messages and lifelines in the diagrams.</li></ol>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=136715002 space=MD2024x version=1 -->
## PAGE 00413: Default visibility of property groups and subgroups

- page_id: `136715002`
- space_key: `MD2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024x/pages/136715002/Default+visibility+of+property+groups+and+subgroups
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-09-20T18:17:00.254+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > UML Profiling and DSL Guide > Domain Specific Language customization > Using Customization Data > Creating Property Groups and Subgroups
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

You can create property groups and subgroups for both standard UML metaclasses and [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Stereotype' space='MD2024x']. The group will be added to either the metaclass or to the stereotype depending on the customization target of the customized property group.

If the customization target is a stereotype and the **Hide Metatype**property is set to true, the specified property group in this customization is visible only for the elements with this stereotype applied.

If the customization target is a metaclass, the specified group in this customization will be visible for the elements of this particular type. This group is also visible for the elements with applied stereotypes, except when the **Hide Metatype**property in this stereotype customization is set to true.

The table below lists the default property group visibility in the element’s [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Specification window' space='MD2024x'].

| Customization target | Property group visibility in element’s Specification window and Properties panel |  |  |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
|  | Element of metaclass type | Element with stereotype with DSL customization Hide Metatype=false | Element with stereotype with DSL customization Hide Metatype=true |
| Stereotype | Invisible | Visible in standard mode | Visible in standard mode |
| Metaclass | Visible in standard mode | Visible in standard mode | Visible in all modes |

#### PANEL: Related Pages

Related Pages

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Creating Customization Data' space='MD2024x']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Using Customization Data' space='MD2024x']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Creating Property Groups and Subgroups' space='MD2024x']

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=136712915 space=MD2024x version=2 -->
## PAGE 00414: Defining Constraint condition

- page_id: `136712915`
- space_key: `MD2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024x/pages/136712915/Defining+Constraint+condition
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2025-05-28T15:19:05.188+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > Model analysis > Validation > Custom validation > Creating new validation rules
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

INLINE

You must specify the validation condition for a validation rule. This condition must be true when evaluated in order for the Constraint to be satisfied. The result of a validation rule must be of the boolean type.

To define a Constraint condition

1. Open the Constraint Specification window. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Specification window' space='']>]]>
2. Find the Specification property and select its value box.
3. Click the [...] button. The Specification dialog opens.
4. From the Language list, select one of the following languages:
  1. OCL 2.0 is used for validation rules specified in the OCL language. **OCL Header**If you select the OCL2.0 language, the header of the expression from the constraint information is generated automatically according to the following rules: context <constrained element> <constraint type> <constraint name if any>:. See the following image.[IMAGE alt='' src='']**Constraint types**Since the Constraint is stereotyped by «validationRule» which is derived from «invariant » stereotype, inv is shown in the header. Only inv constraints can be evaluated. Other types of Constraints are not evaluated, but can be modeled for documentation purposes:**def**– for the expression of the constraint with «definition» stereotype applied.**init, derive**- for the expression of the default value of the property.The **derive** expressions can be evaluated indirectly when the validation rule (**inv** constraint) is referencing the property and the validation rule is evaluated.**pre, post, body**- for the expression of the appropriate fields of operation.**OCL Performance**When evaluating the validation rule defined in OCL language, the validation on the first run can have a delay of 20-30 seconds (depending on the computer performance) while the Java compiler is loading. Subsequent validations will run faster than the first one.If the validation process is run heavily on medium-large projects, increasing the default Java VM size is advisable. By default, the VM size is set to 400MB in the modeling tool; increasing this to 600 (or 800 if the computer has sufficient RAM) might improve the performance.
  2. Binary is used for more advanced expressions not easily expressed in OCL. These expressions are written in Java, compiled, and specified in the modeling tool's classpath. These expressions can then be specified as validation rule expressions.
  3. Scripts of JavaScript, Jython, Groovy , and BeanShell . Learn more about script writing in Creating executable opaque behaviors. Since constraints cannot have parameters, you may skip the information about managing parameters.
  4. StructuredExpression . [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Specifying criteria for querying model' space='']>]]>
5. In the Body box, type the expression of the selected language syntax. The modeling tool can evaluate only those validation rules whose expression is defined in one of the languages listed above. The other languages can be used only for documentation purposes. You can add hyperlinks in the **Body**box. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Defining hyperlinks' space='']>]]>You can define expressions for global validation rules. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Global validation rules' space='']>]]>
6. (Optional) Click the Evaluation Mode button to execute an expression on the actual testing model while editing. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Expression Evaluation' space='']>]]>
7. Click OK . The Constraint condition is defined.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:structured-macro ac:name="excerpt" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="4de8cb5d-9a7e-4bca-a25e-64130f3d8b80"><ac:parameter ac:name="atlassian-macro-output-type">INLINE</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>You must specify the validation condition for a validation rule. This condition must be true when evaluated in order for the Constraint to be satisfied. The result of a validation rule must be of the boolean type.</p><p>To define a Constraint condition</p><hr /><ol><li data-uuid="fde128e1-db0e-4047-974f-473601bd57bd">Open the Constraint <strong>Specification </strong>window. <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Specification window" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[How to open the Specification window >>]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link></li><li data-uuid="73daefd7-6439-49ae-8223-d5db6a3cab62">Find the <strong>Specification</strong> property and select its value box.</li><li data-uuid="e7343483-5834-4c84-84fa-69d50386f6bd">Click the <strong>[...]</strong> button. The <strong>Specification </strong>dialog opens.</li><li data-uuid="55110ea9-1529-47c5-b3d0-70f8e33b4a6b">From the <strong>Language </strong>list, select one of the following languages:<ol><li><strong>OCL 2.0</strong> is used for validation rules specified in the OCL language.<br /><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="33001904-6d9f-4343-96f1-bdbc9e0de79b"><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>OCL Header </strong></p><p>If you select the OCL2.0 language, the header of the expression from the constraint information is generated automatically according to the following rules: context &lt;constrained element&gt; &lt;constraint type&gt; &lt;constraint name if any&gt;:.  See the following image.</p><p><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="specification.png" /></ac:image></p><p><strong>Constraint types </strong></p><p>Since the Constraint is stereotyped by «validationRule» which is derived from «invariant » stereotype, inv is shown in the header. Only inv constraints can be evaluated. Other types of Constraints are not evaluated, but can be modeled for documentation purposes:</p><ul><li><strong>def </strong>– for the expression of the constraint with «definition» stereotype applied.</li><li><strong>init, derive </strong>- for the expression of the default value of the property.</li></ul><ac:structured-macro ac:name="info" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="9d0ff826-a8f7-41fd-ae64-7fd15ed1b889"><ac:rich-text-body><p>The <strong>derive</strong> expressions can be evaluated indirectly when the validation rule (<strong>inv</strong> constraint) is referencing the property and the validation rule is evaluated.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ul><li><strong>pre, post, body </strong>- for the expression of the appropriate fields of operation. </li></ul><p><strong>OCL Performance </strong></p><p>When evaluating the validation rule defined in OCL language, the validation on the first run can have a delay of 20-30 seconds (depending on the computer performance) while the Java compiler is loading. Subsequent validations will run faster than the first one. </p><p>If the validation process is run heavily on medium-large projects, increasing the default Java VM size is advisable. By default, the VM size is set to 400MB in the modeling tool; increasing this to 600 (or 800 if the computer has sufficient RAM) might improve the performance.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></li><li><strong>Binary</strong> is used for more advanced expressions not easily expressed in OCL. These expressions are written in Java, compiled, and specified in the modeling tool's classpath. These expressions can then be specified as validation rule expressions.</li><li>Scripts of <strong>JavaScript, Jython, Groovy</strong>, and <strong>BeanShell</strong>. Learn more about script writing in Creating executable opaque behaviors. Since constraints cannot have parameters, you may skip the information about managing parameters.</li><li><strong>StructuredExpression</strong>. <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Specifying criteria for querying model" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Learn more about specifying criteria for querying model >>]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link></li></ol></li><li data-uuid="d0cc0f8d-07be-4a4a-95d6-7a880bb0ba34">In the <strong>Body </strong>box, type the expression of the selected language syntax.<br /><ac:structured-macro ac:name="warning" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="2b86ab07-bc39-41d5-8eb4-c3080e72e935"><ac:rich-text-body><p>The modeling tool can evaluate only those validation rules whose expression is defined in one of the languages listed above. The other languages can be used only for documentation purposes.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><br /><ac:structured-macro ac:name="info" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="a99b9755-0acf-444c-ad9c-5ea350f97a74"><ac:rich-text-body><p>You can add hyperlinks in the <strong>Body </strong>box. <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Defining hyperlinks" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[How to define hyperlinks >>]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link></p><p>You can define expressions for global validation rules. <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Global validation rules" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Learn more about global validation rules >>]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></li><li data-uuid="f82ea6cb-67c9-4a29-b25d-6ae4d45e9b86">(Optional) Click the <strong>Evaluation Mode </strong>button to execute an expression on the actual testing model while editing. <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Expression Evaluation" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[How to use the Evaluation Mode >>]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link></li><li data-uuid="01cac4b0-4f84-4bb1-abfc-840e3327ec41">Click <strong>OK</strong>. The Constraint condition is defined.</li></ol></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=136715039 space=MD2024x version=1 -->
## PAGE 00415: Defining expressions

- page_id: `136715039`
- space_key: `MD2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024x/pages/136715039/Defining+expressions
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-09-20T18:17:01.340+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > UML Profiling and DSL Guide > Domain Specific Language customization > Using Customization Data > Extending Metamodel with Derived Properties
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

1852034054

1852034059

1852034058

Each derived property must have one or more expressions defining the criteria for searching the result elements.

It is strongly recommended that you specify the customization target in the customization element before defining the expression for its derived property.

To open the dialog for a new expression definition

1. Select the attribute with the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Creating and specifying derived properties' space='MD2024x'].
2. Open the attribute’s [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Specification window' space='MD2024x'].
3. In the general specification pane (opened by default), click the **Expression** property and then click the **...** button. The **Expression**dialog opens, and you can then construct predefined expressions or define your custom operation for calculating derived property values. To learn more about derived properties, see the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Custom traceability rules' space='MD2024x'] page. For more information on expression specification, see the[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Specifying criteria for querying model' space='MD2024x']page.

1852034052

**Related pages**

- Extending Metamodel with Derived Properties

For more information <span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">on expression specification, see the </span><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Specifying criteria for querying model" /></ac:link><span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;"> page.</span></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></li></ol></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="00fbb0df-32d4-4ac3-82f5-986c06e43ce0"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1852034052</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Related pages</strong></p><ul><li class="ancestor-link parent-link"><a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MD2024x/Extending+Metamodel+with+Derived+Properties">Extending Metamodel with Derived Properties</a></li></ul><p><br /></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>

## PAGE 00416: Defining hyperlinks

- page_id: `136711704`
- space_key: `MD2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024x/pages/136711704/Defining+hyperlinks
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2024-01-30T10:15:53.284+01:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > Working with model elements > Manage Navigation
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

754380129

754380132

1428983844

**On this page**

32

754380131

INLINE

#### Introduction

The hyperlink is a reference to another location that you access by clicking a highlighted text or element. Hyperlinks ensure fast access to information directly from your model. You can create hyperlinks in text, on elements, or on the entire diagram to link them to external or internal resources. For example, a hyperlink can open a file from your file system. The following figure illustrates all available hyperlink types.

The hyperlinks to OSLC resources are available only if the Cameo DataHub Plugin is installed. Please see[how to install the Cameo DataHub Plugin >>](https://docs.nomagic.com/display/CDH2024x/Installation)

###### [IMAGE alt='' src='']Hyperlink types in modeling tools.

#### Hyperlinks representation

Visual representation of hyperlinks is outlined below:

#### Adding hyperlinks

For more information on how to add and manage hyperlinks, please see:

2

- Linking model elements to OSLC resources

754380128

**Related pages**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Manage Navigation' space='MD2024x']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Working with model elements' space='MD2024x']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='HTML editor' space='MD2024x']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Managing hyperlinks' space='MD2024x']

Hyperlinks ensure fast access to information directly from your model. You can create hyperlinks in text, on elements, or on the entire diagram to link them to external or internal resources. For example, a hyperlink can open a file from your file system. The following figure illustrates all available hyperlink types.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="warning" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="adccf501-3612-4cd8-a12d-2f4cdd547da8"><ac:rich-text-body><p>The hyperlinks to OSLC resources are available only if the Cameo DataHub Plugin is installed. Please see<a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/CDH2024x/Installation" rel="nofollow"> how to install the Cameo DataHub Plugin &gt;&gt;</a></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><h6 style="text-align: center;"><ac:image ac:align="center" ac:queryparams="effects=border-simple,shadow-kn"><ri:attachment ri:filename="hyperlinks_illustration.png" /></ac:image>Hyperlink types in modeling tools.</h6><h2>Hyperlinks representation</h2><p>Visual representation of hyperlinks is outlined below:</p><ul><ul><li>Hyperlink in text is underlined and blue. For example, <ac:image ac:thumbnail="true" ac:height="16"><ri:attachment ri:filename="hyperlink_on_element_in_model_browser.png" /></ac:image>, <ac:image ac:thumbnail="true" ac:height="20"><ri:attachment ri:filename="hyperlink_on_diagram_in_model_browser.png" /></ac:image>.<br />         - On the diagram pane, on the symbol, the icon representing the subject to which the hyperlink is added appears. For example:<br />                      - To a webpage:<br />                                 <ac:image ac:thumbnail="true" ac:height="59"><ri:attachment ri:filename="hyperlink_to_webpage.png" /></ac:image><br />                      - To a file:<br />                                   <ac:image ac:thumbnail="true" ac:height="69"><ri:attachment ri:filename="hyperlink_to_pdf.png" /></ac:image><br />                      - To an element or diagram:<br />                                     <ac:image ac:thumbnail="true" ac:height="48"><ri:attachment ri:filename="hyperlink_to_ibd.png" /></ac:image> <ac:image ac:thumbnail="true" ac:height="49"><ri:attachment ri:filename="hyperlink_to_block.png" /></ac:image><br />                      - To OSLC resources, wizards, validation, and reports:<br />                                      <ac:image ac:thumbnail="true" ac:height="65"><ri:attachment ri:filename="hyperlink_to_oslc_wizards_validation_reports.png" /></ac:image></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="a98e1b75-a0ea-4c29-a2e2-1c8b6c7d9669"><ac:rich-text-body><p><span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);">If the element has an active navigation target then an active navigation target icon is show on a symbol. You can choose whether to show the icon by changing a value of the <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Customizing environment options" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Environment Option]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> <strong>Show Active Navigation Icons</strong>. By default, this option is set to <em>true</em>. </span></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></li></ul></ul><h2 class="confluence-link"><span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);">Adding hyperlinks<br /></span></h2><p class="confluence-link"><span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);">For more information on how to add and manage hyperlinks, please see:</span></p><p class="confluence-link"><ac:structured-macro ac:name="children" ac:schema-version="2" ac:macro-id="ae49459b-75ed-4bf2-b663-f60525fd97db"><ac:parameter ac:name="depth">2</ac:parameter></ac:structured-macro></p><ul><li><a class="current" href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MD2024x/Linking+model+elements+to+OSLC+resources" rel="nofollow">Linking model elements to OSLC resources</a></li></ul><p><br /></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="46ae756c-d3da-48fc-a566-9191c406428d"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">754380128</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p class="auto-cursor-target"><strong>Related pages</strong></p><ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Manage Navigation" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Working with model elements" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="HTML editor" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Managing hyperlinks" /></ac:link></li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=136710730 space=MD2024x version=2 -->
## PAGE 00417: Defining layout template

- page_id: `136710730`
- space_key: `MD2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024x/pages/136710730/Defining+layout+template
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2024-02-01T11:42:27.863+01:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > Diagramming > Layout templates
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

#### CONTENT-LAYER: Composite Structure Diagram as a layout template

57880485

#### CONTENT-COLUMN: Composite Structure Diagram as a layout template

57880487

#### CONTENT-BLOCK: Composite Structure Diagram as a layout template

57880486

If you want to reuse the style and layout of Class with its Ports in another diagram, you need to define the appearance of the Class, the style, and position of its Ports in the Class diagram. It means that you define a new layout template. When defining the appearance of the Class, you can change its color, font, size, layout, and other style properties. To do this, follow the procedure below.

#### WARNING: Important

Important

Only one Class shape with its Ports can be defined in one layout template diagram.

To define the layout template

1. Create the Class diagram. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Creating diagrams' space='MD2024x']]]>
2. Create a new or represent an existing Class with its Ports on the diagram pane.
3. Specify the appearance of all shapes by using the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Symbol Properties dialog' space='MD2024x']Symbol Properties dialog and [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Understanding the user interface' space='MD2024x'] .
4. Set the positions of Ports on Class shape manually. 
The appearance of Class and its Ports shapes are defined (see the figure below). [IMAGE alt='' src='']The comparison of default and defined appearance of the Modem Card Block, style, and the position of its ports. The illustration displays concepts from SysML Plugin.

#### INFO: Composite Structure Diagram as a layout template

Composite Structure Diagram as a layout template

- From the version 19.0 SP1, you can define the appearance and layout of a Part Property with its Ports in the Composite Structure Diagram and set this diagram as layout template.
- If a Composite Structure Diagram displays several Part Properties, [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Creating layout template' space='MD2024x'] directly from a particular Part Property. It's appearance and layout will be copied to the template automatically.

57880484

**Sample model**

The sample model used in the figure of this page is the **Modem Cable** sample model. To open this sample do one of the following:

- Download *[Modem Cable Layout Templates.mdzip](https://docs.nomagic.com/download/attachments/9915051/Modem%20Cable%20Layout%20Templates.mdzip?version=1&modificationDate=1481817717837&api=v2).*

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-layer" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="8bb0a2e7-892b-4242-80e5-86b9bf406d90"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">57880485</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-column" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="9bc8f9dc-2762-4735-a9ca-552adfd292fc"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">57880487</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="2b744567-a57e-4b03-bb63-38e4e36b3a00"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">57880486</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>If you want to reuse the style and layout of Class with its Ports in another diagram, you need to define the appearance of the Class, the style, and position of its Ports in the Class diagram. It means that you define a new layout template. When defining the appearance of the Class, you can change its color, font, size, layout, and other style properties. To do this, follow the procedure below.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="warning" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="e8579265-ff01-4631-a7bb-2ec607d5bbd0"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Important</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>Only one Class shape with its Ports can be defined in one layout template diagram.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p>To define the layout template</p><hr /><ol><li>Create the Class diagram. <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Creating diagrams" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[How to create diagrams >]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link></li><li>Create a new or represent an existing Class with its Ports on the diagram pane.</li><li>Specify the appearance of all shapes by using the <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Symbol Properties dialog" /><ac:link-body><span class="confluence-link">Symbol Properties</span> dialog</ac:link-body></ac:link> and <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Understanding the user interface" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[diagram toolbar]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>.</li><li><p>Set the positions of Ports on Class shape manually.<br />The appearance of Class and its Ports shapes are defined (see the figure below).<br /><br /></p><h6 style="text-align: center;"><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="comparison_of_default_and_user_defined_appearance_of_block_and_ports.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Defining layout template" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><span style="color: rgb(51,51,51);"> </span>The comparison of default and defined appearance of the Modem Card Block, style, and the position of its ports. The illustration displays concepts from SysML Plugin.</h6></li></ol><ac:structured-macro ac:name="info" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="136bf21e-c771-40e3-b5f7-62d0f1b41818"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Composite Structure Diagram as a layout template</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ul><li>From the version 19.0 SP1, you can define the appearance and layout of a Part Property with its Ports in the Composite Structure Diagram and set this diagram as layout template.</li><li>If a Composite Structure Diagram displays several Part Properties, <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Creating layout template" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[create a layout template]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> directly from a particular Part Property. It's appearance and layout will be copied to the template automatically.</li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="c7d43643-3e58-4124-a1d3-af337aa2d4ad"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">57880484</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p class="auto-cursor-target"><strong>Sample model</strong></p><p>The sample model used in the figure of this page is the <strong>Modem Cable</strong> sample model. To open this sample do one of the following:</p><ul><li>Download <span class="confluence-link" style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;"><em><a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/download/attachments/9915051/Modem%20Cable%20Layout%20Templates.mdzip?version=1&amp;modificationDate=1481817717837&amp;api=v2">Modem Cable Layout Templates.mdzip</a>.</em></span></li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=136714977 space=MD2024x version=1 -->
## PAGE 00418: Defining Preferred Metatype

- page_id: `136714977`
- space_key: `MD2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024x/pages/136714977/Defining+Preferred+Metatype
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-09-20T18:16:59.161+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > UML Profiling and DSL Guide > Domain Specific Language customization > Using Customization Data
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

The DSL customization engine allows specification of a preferred metatype if more than one metatype is defined for the DSL element.

To define a preferred metatype for the DSL type

1. Select the appropriate [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Customization Elements' space='MD2024x'] and open its [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Specification window' space='MD2024x'] .
2. Click the value specification cell of the Preferred Metatype property and do one of the following: The selected metatype will be assigned as the **Preferred Metatype** property value. falseMultiple selection is not allowed. You must define a single value.
  - [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Selecting single property value from editable list' space='MD2024x'] and close the Specification window.
  - Click the [ATTACHMENT filename='edit.png'] button [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Creating new elements' space='MD2024x'] . In the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Selecting elements' space='MD2024x'] , select the created metatype and click OK. Close the Specification window.

After updating the customization element specification, you must either [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Using other projects in a project' space='MD2024x'] in the model or reopen the model.

From now on, all DSL elements will be created with the preferred metatype specified in the customization.

#### PANEL: Related Pages

Related Pages

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Creating Customization Data' space='MD2024x']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Using Customization Data' space='MD2024x']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html

In the <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Selecting elements" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[element Selection dialog]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>, select the created metatype and click OK. Close the Specification window.</li></ul><p class="auto-cursor-target">The selected metatype will be assigned as the <strong>Preferred Metatype</strong> property value.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="d15e29e9-b1b6-4318-af4e-392c34391b6f"><ac:parameter ac:name="icon">false</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>Multiple selection is not allowed. You must define a single value.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></li></ol><p><span><span>After updating the customization element specification, you must either <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Using other projects in a project" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[reuse the DSL Customization profile]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> in the model or reopen the model.</span><br /></span></p><p>From now on, all DSL elements will be created with the preferred metatype specified in the customization.</p></div></div></ac:layout-cell><ac:layout-cell><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="panel" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="68d6f843-19aa-480d-81e1-df3547d11a1b"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Related Pages</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Creating Customization Data" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Using Customization Data" /></ac:link></li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section></ac:layout>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=136712111 space=MD2024x version=2 -->
## PAGE 00419: Defining properties of the customized element to be spell checked

- page_id: `136712111`
- space_key: `MD2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024x/pages/136712111/Defining+properties+of+the+customized+element+to+be+spell+checked
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2024-01-30T15:09:43.560+01:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > Tools > Spelling Checker
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

266235839

266235842

266235840

You can check both DSL customization classes and their properties. You can choose which properties of the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Customization Elements' space='MD2024x'] ([CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Class' space='MD2024x']with a «Customization» [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Stereotype' space='MD2024x']) to be spell checked.

You accomplish this by defining a customization class [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Editing tagged value' space='MD2024x']*checkSpelling* value, found in the properties tag group. By creating a value for this tag, you can choose String properties to check spelling for. By default, there are no properties marked as checkable.

266235838

**Related pages**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Editing tagged value' space='']

[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Spelling Checker' space='']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

<ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-layer" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="a708c5c7-685d-44fa-9dfe-ccb51aaabee5"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">266235839</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-column" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="9dd85cf0-ceb5-4bb3-ae3e-925a88dc986c"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">266235842</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="10825313-6dcd-4639-9359-d6d3b8437661"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">266235840</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>You can check both DSL customization classes and their properties. You can choose which properties of the <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Customization Elements" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[customized element]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> (<ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Class" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Class ]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>with a «Customization» <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Stereotype" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[stereotype]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>) to be spell checked.</p><p>You accomplish this by defining a customization class <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Editing tagged value" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[tag ]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link><em>checkSpelling</em> value, found in the properties tag group. By creating a value for this tag, you can choose String properties to check spelling for. By default, there are no properties marked as checkable.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="ed53d2da-e3fe-4ff5-8a9b-441db71f078c"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">266235838</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Related pages</strong></p><ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Editing tagged value" /></ac:link><strong><br class="_mce_tagged_br" /></strong></li></ul><p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="children" ac:schema-version="2" ac:macro-id="6a403500-73c2-4d8a-89cb-da21da2619d1"><ac:parameter ac:name="page"><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Spelling Checker" /></ac:link></ac:parameter></ac:structured-macro></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=136715715 space=MD2024x version=1 -->
## PAGE 00420: Deleting and executing Macros

- page_id: `136715715`
- space_key: `MD2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024x/pages/136715715/Deleting+and+executing+Macros
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-09-20T18:17:42.615+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > Macro Engine Documentation
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

851813206

851813217

851813204

**On this page:**

851813216

You can click the **[IMAGE alt='' src='']** or **[IMAGE alt='' src='']** button in the **Organize Macros** dialog to delete or execute a selected macro. You can also press the pre-designed mnemonic keys to delete or run a macro as described in****Organizing Macros Dialog Mnemonic Keys below.

##### Deleting a Macro

To delete a macro from the **Organize Macros** dialog

1. Click Tools > Macros > Organize Macros to open the Organize Macros dialog.
2. Select a macro from the table and either click Delete or press Alt + D . A dialog will open, asking whether you want to delete the macro, see figure below.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

3. Click **Yes** and the macro will be deleted from the **Organize Macros** dialog.

##### Executing a Macro

To execute a macro from the **Organize Macros** dialog

1. Click Tools > Macros > Organize Macros The Organize Macros dialog will open, see figure below. [ATTACHMENT filename='organize-macros.png']
2. Select a macro from the table and click [IMAGE alt='' src=''] . After the macro has been executed, a message will open: The macro <macro name> has been executed .
3. If you have the parameters in the **Macro Information** dialog, you need to specify the value in the **Macro Arguments** dialog, see figure below, before running the macro. For an array datatype, you need to click the [IMAGE alt='' src=''] button in the **Value** column in the **Macro Arguments** dialog and enter each value into each line. The value of the first line will be the value in array index 0.An ElementPath is a Qualified Name. You can find this information in the specification dialog of each element, see figure below.The **Macro Arguments** dialog, see the figure below, will be displayed if the**[IMAGE alt='' src='']** check box is not selected.If you want to save argument values in the **Macro Arguments** dialog, you need to select the **Set as default values** check box before you click **OK**.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

- You can also execute a macro from the main browser in MagicDraw by pressing the shortcut keys that you have defined in the Organize Macros dialog.
- You can only run macro one at a time.
- If there is an error while running a macro, for example, syntax error, the following message will open: MagicDraw cannot execute the <macro language> macro, please make sure that <path, filename, extension> is correct. <error description> .
- If MagicDraw cannot find a macro file in the location that you have specified in the Open dialog, the following message will open: MagicDraw cannot find the macro: <path, filename, extension> .

##### Organizing Macros Dialog Mnemonic Keys

Macro Engine also provides mnemonic keys to add, edit, delete, and run a macro from the **Organize Macros** dialog. The table below shows the **Organize Macros** dialog mnemonic keys and their function.

*Table listing of Organize Macros Dialog Mnemonic Keys*

| Mnemonic keys | Button | Function |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Alt + A |  | To add a macro in the Macro Information dialog. |
| Alt + E |  | To edit a macro in the Macro Information dialog. |
| Alt + D |  | To delete a macro from the Organize Macros dialog. |
| Alt + O |  | Code To edit source code in Macro Editor. |
| Alt + R |  | To run a macro from the Organize Macros dialog. |
| Alt + C |  | To click the Close button. |
| Alt + H |  | To click the Help button. |

- You can click the [IMAGE alt='' src=''] button in the Organize Macros dialog, see figure above, to edit and save source code in the Macro Editor dialog, see figure below.
- You can click [IMAGE alt='' src=''] to save the source code or click [IMAGE alt='' src=''] to run the macro in the Macro Editor dialog, see figure below.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-layer" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="ba6abb75-42b8-4f40-b744-30b4aebb4d06"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">851813206</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-column" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="91306ecd-be05-4444-af78-d85e7ccae0f6"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">851813217</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="699757f1-8046-490b-8727-712e93a1ec1c"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">851813204</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>On this page:</strong></p><p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="toc" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="4d22fc56-6ce2-4584-b32f-3fe6de78e1f8" /></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="c389014a-0684-4581-aab3-8cd60ace66b4"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">851813216</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>You can click the <strong><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="delete-btn.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Deleting and executing Macros" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></strong> or <strong><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="run-btn.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Deleting and executing Macros" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></strong> button in the <strong>Organize Macros</strong> dialog to delete or execute a selected macro. After the macro has been executed, a message will open: <strong>The macro &lt;macro name&gt; has been executed</strong>.</li><li><p>If you have the parameters in the <strong>Macro Information</strong> dialog, you need to specify the value in the <strong>Macro Arguments</strong> dialog, see figure below, before running the macro.<br /><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="info" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="d6220ff6-7095-4a08-8acc-baf1ecd3b9b3"><ac:rich-text-body><ul><li>For an array datatype, you need to click the <ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="button-1.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Deleting and executing Macros" /></ri:attachment></ac:image> button in the <strong>Value</strong> column in the <strong>Macro Arguments</strong> dialog and enter each value into each line. You can find this information in the specification dialog of each element, see figure below.</li><li>The <strong>Macro Arguments</strong> dialog, see the figure below, will be displayed if the<strong> <ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="automatically-run-with-defaults.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Deleting and executing Macros" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></strong> check box is not selected.</li><li>If you want to save argument values in the <strong>Macro Arguments</strong> dialog, you need to select the <strong>Set as default values</strong> check box before you click <strong>OK</strong>.</li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></li></ol><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image ac:title="Specification of class" ac:alt="Specification of class"><ri:attachment ri:filename="spec-of-class.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Deleting and executing Macros" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image ac:title="Macro Arguments Dialog" ac:alt="Macro Arguments Dialog"><ri:attachment ri:filename="fig 17.PNG"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Deleting and executing Macros" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="info" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="62b27853-7ba5-4395-9c7d-9b9929311c76"><ac:rich-text-body><ul><li>You can also execute a macro from the main browser in MagicDraw by pressing the shortcut keys that you have defined in the <strong>Organize Macros</strong> dialog.</li><li>You can only run macro one at a time.</li><li>If there is an error while running a macro, for example, syntax error, the following message will open: <strong>MagicDraw cannot execute the &lt;macro language&gt; can click the <strong><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="edit-code.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Deleting and executing Macros" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></strong> button in the <strong>Organize Macros</strong> dialog, see figure above, to edit and save source code in the <strong>Macro Editor</strong> dialog, see figure below.</li><li>You can click <strong><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="save-btn.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Deleting and executing Macros" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></strong> to save the source code or click <strong><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="run-btn.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Deleting and executing Macros" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></strong> to run the macro in the <strong>Macro Editor</strong> dialog, see figure below.</li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image ac:title="Macro Editor" ac:alt="Macro Editor"><ri:attachment ri:filename="fig 15.PNG"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Deleting and executing Macros" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=136715219 space=MD2024x version=2 -->
## PAGE 00421: Deleting presentation elements

- page_id: `136715219`
- space_key: `MD2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024x/pages/136715219/Deleting+presentation+elements
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2024-02-13T08:41:58.215+01:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > Developer Guide > Working with diagrams > Presentation elements (symbols) > Working with presentation elements
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

To remove the given *com.nomagic.magicdraw.uml.symbols.PresentationElement* from the diagram, use the method *com.nomagic.magicdraw.openapi.uml.PresentationElementsManager.deletePresentationElement(**PresentationElement)*:

```java

```

All related presentation elements with all children and connected paths will be removed too.

#### PANEL: Related pages

Related pages

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Session management' space='']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:layout><ac:layout-section ac:type="two_right_sidebar"><ac:layout-cell><p>To remove the given <em>com.nomagic.magicdraw.uml.symbols.PresentationElement</em> from the diagram, use the method <em>com.nomagic.magicdraw.openapi.uml.PresentationElementsManager.deletePresentationElement(</em><em>PresentationElement)</em>:</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="fac020d6-9cfa-4b60-8e68-3b1eea64df87"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">java</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[    PresentationElement element = ...; 
    SessionManager.getInstance().createSession("Test"); 
    PresentationElementsManager.getInstance().removePresentationElement(element); 
    SessionManager.getInstance().closeSession();]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p>All related presentation elements with all children and connected paths will be removed too.</p></ac:layout-cell><ac:layout-cell><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="panel" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="21356239-0c67-43fd-9dd9-2ffb96badd06"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Related pages</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Session management" /></ac:link></li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><br /></p><p><br /></p><p><br /></p><p><br /></p><p><br /></p></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section></ac:layout>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=136710992 space=MD2024x version=2 -->
## PAGE 00422: Dependency

- page_id: `136710992`
- space_key: `MD2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024x/pages/136710992/Dependency
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2024-01-30T14:53:41.652+01:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > UML elements
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

#### CONTENT-LAYER: Editing property values

745065613

#### CONTENT-COLUMN: Editing property values

745065615

#### CONTENT-BLOCK: Editing property values

745065614

A dependency is a relationship signifying that a [model element](https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MD2024x/Model+elements) requires other model elements for its specification or implementation. The client element is either semantically or structurally dependent on the definition of the supplier element(s).

A dependency is shown as a dashed arrow between classes or packages. The client element (at the tail of the arrow) depends on the supplier element (at the arrowhead). You can label the arrow with an optional stereotype and an individual name.

You can also draw a dependency between a [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Class' space='MD2024x'] and other Class elements, such as [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Attribute' space='MD2024x'] and [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Operation' space='MD2024x'].

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Example of dependency relationships: «access» and «import».

Dependency, [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Navigating between different levels of abstraction' space='MD2024x'], and [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Analyzing usages and dependencies' space='MD2024x'] are defined in the dialog of the same structure. They differ from one another only by the corresponding Specification name. You can specify a dependency by changing its property values in the dependency [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Specification window' space='MD2024x']. Each property is described in the description area on this window.

#### TIP: Editing property values

Editing property values

For more information about specifying property values, see [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Editing property values' space='MD2024x'].

There are several kinds of dependencies:

| Dependency kind | Description |
| --- | --- |
| Template binding | Represents a relationship between a templatable element and a template. A template binding specifies the substitutions of actual parameters for the formal parameters of the template.You can specify template binding dependency properties and find each property's description in the template binding dependency Specification window. Descriptions are presented in the description area of the Specification window. |
| Abstraction | Relates two elements or sets of elements that represent the same concept at different levels of abstraction or from different viewpoints. In the metamodel, an abstraction is a dependency in which there is a mapping between the supplier and the client.Define an abstraction relationship in the Abstraction Specification window. |
| Usage | A relationship in which one element requires another element (or set of elements) for its full implementation or . In the metamodel, a usage is a dependency in which the client requires the presence of the supplier.Define a usage relationship in the Usage Specification window. |
| Package merge | A directed relationship between two , indicating that the contents of the two packages are to be combined. It has a dependency relationship with the applied stereotype «merge».Define a merge relationship in the Dependency Specification window. |
| Element import | A directed relationship between an importing namespace and a packageable element. The name of the packageable element or its alias is added to the namespace of the importing namespace. It has a dependency relationship with the applied stereotype «import».Define an import relationship in the Dependency Specification window. To draw the Element Import link, select the Element Import path to draw in the Class diagram toolbar from the Abstraction group. |
| Access | Shows that elements can only be accessed from a , and it cannot be referenced. |
| Deployment | The allocation of a deployment target to an artifact or artifact instance. |
| Role Binding | A relationship in a . Role Binding is used to connect with . |
| Mount | A mount dependency represents a former location of a shared package; that is, a location where the package was mounted in a local project.A mount dependency is created:After you export a project from Teamwork Cloud; orWhen a local project is added to Teamwork Cloud.Mounted packages in the Containment tree are adorned with an icon and have their project usage name in brackets, as shown in the following figure:The purpose of establishing the mount dependency is to ensure the same containment structure during project transformation to Teamwork Cloud, to maintain the correct scope in utilities like smart packages, validation rules, tables etc. |

745065612

**Related pages**

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-layer" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="f239384e-b503-4fb4-9f1e-b7211948cf8b"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">745065613</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-column" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="b8e87a6e-081f-4e53-812d-9265a4881a1b"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">745065615</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="6eab5bf6-1140-4dc9-8eeb-0a1f913f5911"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">745065614</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>A dependency is a relationship signifying that a <a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MD2024x/Model+elements">model element</a> requires other model elements for its specification or implementation. The client element is either semantically or structurally dependent on the definition of the supplier element(s).</p><p>A dependency is shown as a dashed arrow between classes or packages. The client element (at the tail of the arrow) depends on the supplier element (at the arrowhead). You can label the arrow with an optional stereotype and an individual name.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="478fd602-4249-41b7-b0b9-3453d0416c17"><ac:rich-text-body><p>You can also draw a dependency between a <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Class" /></ac:link> and other Class elements, such as <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Attribute" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[attributes]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> and <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Operation" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[operations]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p> <ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="example_of_dependencies.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Dependency" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">Example of dependency relationships: «access» and «import».</h6><p>Dependency, <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Navigating between different levels of abstraction" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[abstraction]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>, and <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Analyzing usages and dependencies" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[usage relationships]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> are defined in the dialog of the same structure. They differ from one another only by the corresponding Specification name. You can specify a dependency by changing its property values in the dependency <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Specification window" /></ac:link>. Each property is described in the description area on this window.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="tip" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="be0793a2-8907-4c1a-bc47-4d1ec1f673c3"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Editing property values</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><span style="color: rgb(0,0,0);">For more information about specifying property values, see <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Editing property values" /></ac:link>.</span></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p><p>There are several kinds of dependencies:</p><table class="wrapped"><colgroup><col /><col /></colgroup><tbody><tr><th>Dependency kind</th><th>Description</th></tr><tr><td><h4>Template binding</h4></td><td><div class="content-wrapper"><p>Represents a relationship between a templatable element and a template. A template binding specifies the substitutions of actual parameters for the formal parameters of the template.</p><p>You can specify template binding dependency properties and find each property's description in the template binding dependency Specification window. Descriptions are presented in the description area of the Specification window. </p></div></td></tr><tr><td><h4>Abstraction</h4></td><td><div class="content-wrapper"><p>Relates two elements or sets of elements that represent the same concept at different levels of abstraction or from different viewpoints. In the metamodel, an abstraction is a dependency in which there is a mapping between the supplier and the client.</p><p>Define an abstraction relationship in the Abstraction Specification window. </p></div></td></tr><tr><td><h4>Usage</h4></td><td><p>A relationship in which one element requires another element (or set of elements) for its full implementation or <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Operation" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[operation]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>. In the metamodel, a usage is a dependency in which the client requires the presence of the supplier.</p><p>Define a usage relationship in the Usage Specification window. </p></td></tr><tr><td><h4>Package merge</h4></td><td><p>A directed relationship between two <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Package" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[packages]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>, indicating that the contents of the two packages are to be combined. It has a dependency relationship with the applied stereotype <em>«merge»</em>.</p><p>Define a merge relationship in the Dependency Specification window. </p></td></tr><tr><td colspan="1"><h4>Element import</h4></td><td colspan="1"><div class="content-wrapper"><p>A directed relationship between an importing namespace and a packageable <span style="line-height: 1.42857;">element. The name of the packageable element or its alias is added to the namespace of the importing </span>namespace. It has a dependency relationship with the applied stereotype <em>«import»</em>.</p><p>Define an import relationship in the Dependency Specification window. </p><p>To draw the Element Import link, select the Element Import path to draw in the Class diagram toolbar from the Abstraction group. </p><p><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="element_import.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Dependency" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p></div></td></tr><tr><td colspan="1"><h4>Access</h4></td><td colspan="1"><p>Shows that elements can only be accessed from a <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Package" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[package]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>, and it cannot be referenced.</p></td></tr><tr><td colspan="1"><h4>Deployment</h4></td><td colspan="1"><p>The allocation of a deployment target to an artifact or artifact instance.</p></td></tr><tr><td colspan="1"><h4><span>Role Binding </span></h4></td><td colspan="1"><p>A relationship in a <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Composite Structure diagram" /></ac:link>. Role Binding is used to connect <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Part" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Parts]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> with <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Collaboration Use" /></ac:link>.</p></td></tr><tr><td colspan="1"><div class="content-wrapper"><h4>Mount</h4></div></td><td colspan="1"><div class="content-wrapper"><p>A mount dependency represents a former location of a shared package; that is, a location where the package was mounted in a local project.</p><p>A mount dependency is created:</p><ul><li>After you export a project from Teamwork Cloud; or</li><li><p class="auto-cursor-target">When a local project is added to Teamwork Cloud.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="tip" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="41e837f4-1df3-443e-b319-ad93d7c8ca3c"><ac:rich-text-body><p><span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);">Mounted packages in the Containment tree are <ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="a58e89da-d5aa-4ecd-aa7e-a435ec3ba003">adorned</ac:inline-comment-marker> with an icon and have their project usage name in brackets, <span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);">as shown in the following figure:</span></span></p><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Mounted_projects.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Dependency" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></li></ul><p><span><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="a1597fed-3bf8-4a17-95a7-b2b374fabeb2">The purpose of establishing the mount dependency is to ensure the same containment structure during project transformation to Teamwork Cloud,</ac:inline-comment-marker></span> to maintain the correct scope in utilities like smart packages, validation rules, tables etc.</p></div></td></tr></tbody></table></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="be47bb6c-88f1-4d4c-a33c-ab83bd4e7f8e"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">745065612</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Related pages</strong></p><p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="children" ac:schema-version="2" ac:macro-id="bd2ff3fa-fb34-4aae-b1c3-623e987341db" /></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>
